Ahnfeltia plicata: Bedrock ledges, outcrops and wide gullies which were algal dominated. Delesseria sanguinea frequent with decreasing depth. First small Laminaria hyperborea, with an understroey of Plocamium cartilagineum and Hypoglossum hypoglossoides. One small patch of Haliclona oculata. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Horizontal surfaces of large boulders and bedrock. Irregular bedrock with shallow gullies in kelp forest. Understorey of red weeds e.g. Delessaria sanguinea and large patches of Polyclinum aurantium. This soon gave way to more broken ground with deeper gullies and vertical edges of bedrock with fewer red weeds and a very rich animal turf. All species recorded for the area were present with no obviuos habitat preference. Occasional flat topped plateux, about 1 - 2 m above the general bottom and had sparse kelp cover with a more varied understorey of red weeds and faunal turf. Undercut edges had numerous crabs and a few lobstersand also one leopard spotted goby. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Small rocks were covered with algae, mixed with some Halecium spp. Flatter rocks were covered with Polydora spp. and silt. Clavelina lepadiformis forms many small clumps on many small rocks. There were pebbles with Pomatoceros spp. Sand and gravel was colonised with Sabella spp. and Lanice conchilega (but not very many). Boulders have more bryozoa (Flustra foliacea and small tufts of Bicellaria ciliata, Crisia spp. and Bugula spp.) than red algae. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.
Aplidium proliferum: Tops of outcrops had tatty kelp, the plants smaller than 1 m were overgrown with Obelia spp. and Membranipora mambranacea. The stipes were encrusted with Electra pilosa and Halichondria panicea and red algae. Areas between the kelp with a range of turf species and encrusting species. There were large patches of Myxilla incrustans and Halichondria panicea. Other species included Clavelina lepadiformis, Aplidium spp. and Botrylus schlosseri common smaller species included Crisia spp. and Hiatella arctica. Noticeably fewer Alcyonium digitatum and Flustra foliacea than in deeper sites and more sponges. The sides of the outcrops had fewer algae than elsewhere. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Aplidium proliferum: Bedrock platform arranged in terraces. The dominant attached animal was Alcyonium digitatum quite deeply covering the horizontal and vertical surfaces. Also common were Flustra foliacea, Bugula flabellata and Alcyonidium spp. and a dense turf that seemed to be dominated by Crisia eburnea. Tubularia spp. stalks were in evidence; also very noticeable were the large numbers of amphipod whips, especially protruding from the vertical faces. Also protruding from vertical rocks - crevice were arms of Ophiothrix fragilis in large numbers. On the horizontal tops, but especially on flatter, low profile bedrock Cliona celata (boring phase) was common, otherwise there were very few sponges. Diphasia spp. was common in clumps (like Halecium spp. formed on the south side of the penninsula). Anemones very rare; sponges - ditto (except Cliona spp. and some Leucosolenia spp.). A good variety of crustaceans. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Cobbles with pebbles and silt supporting Laminaria saccharrina and foliose red algae. Difficult to get a good impression of the site due to extremely poor visibility. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Broken rock platform consisting of lumps (of rock) >1 m wide, flat topped, with gullies and channels between. The channels were up to 0.5 m deep. In the base of channel, on the bedrock were pebbles, fine gravel and silt. A layer of flocculents ilt was trapped over the whole surface of the rock. No seaweeds were seen and the rock was dominated by patches of Flustra foliacea and other bryozoans. Alcyonium digitatum and Asterias rubens were abundant. A few other bryozoans and hydroids were found but surveying was difficult due to extremely poor visibility. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Large patches of Alcyonidium diaphanum and Laminaria hyperborea on large boulders. The sides of the boulders were covered in sea- squirts such as Botryllus schlosseri, Morchelium argus, Aplidium proliferatum common. Also Crisia eburnea, Bugula flabellata and Halecium spp.. Small boulders have Laminaria saccharina (presumably less stable boulders). NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Laminaria hyperborea on part of a reef/terrace system. Tatty, small plants with small fronds. The rocks covered with Delesseria sanguinea and Ploacmium cartilagineum and this algae was densely overgrown with Electra pilosa. Kelp stipes again overgrown with a similar biota. The habitat seemed subject to strong wave action as there were rock mills present. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Uppermost parts with a few kelp plants and some red algae, especialy Phyllophora spp., Schottera nicaeensis and Rhodymenia holmsii at the bases of the outcrops. The vertical surfaces were dominated by a faunal turf. Crisia spp. were abundant and covered substantial areas. Polyclinum spp. was also abundant. Aplidium punctum was noticeably more common than on horizontal surfaces . Aplidium proliferum was also common. Myxilla incrustans was also conspicuous in places. Bugula spp. present (especially Bguula turbinata). Few hydroid varieties but Alcyonium digitatum rare at this depth/site. Numerous Ophiothrix fragilis hidden within the tops of the crevices. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Aplidium proliferum: A horizontal cleft typically 1.5 - 2 m above the sea bed and up to 2 m deep into the rock. Sometimes flat ledge forming the lower side of the cleft with silty surface and Urticina felina and Haliclona oculata on it with some Alcyonidium spp. Outer top of the cleft sponge dominated with some Tubularia spp. The cleft was rich in mobile fauna - squat lobster, lobster, edible crabs - many of a large size. Also a leopard spotted goby, and a Yarrels Blenny and a good sized cod seen going into one hole. The lower vertical surface had a thin turf layer, many Hiatella spp., the lowest half metre was scoured clean - often with a rounded profile. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Aplidium proliferum: Rough ground of chalk bedrock plain with boulders, pebbles and cobbles. Current swept and the greatest cover was by red foliose and filamentous algae (e.g. Plocamium cartilagineum). These grew on the tops of smaller cobbles and boulders. Some of the larger boulders in particular were animal dominated. A faunal turf varying from clumps of Flustra foliacea, to growths of Alcyonidium spp. in various forms ranging from long, thin to considerably branched. Botryllus schlosseri also very common, either growing as a crust or as free lobes. Bryozoan turf - especially Bugula spp. Hydroids included Halecium spp. Clavelina lepadiformis also common. Very few sponges (except Leucosolenia) or anemones (except Urticina in sheltered pockets ). Loose, mobile pebbles, were widespread, remaining mostly uncolonised except for Pomatoceros spp. and filamentous algae - small growths. Few fish - no wrasse or cod family. Plaice were most conspicuous and a few others associated in holes. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Horizontal bedrock surface with a dense cover of Flustra foliacea grading to equally dense cover of Polydora spp. tubes at the base of the next ledge; lots of sterias rubens and a fewer Henricia spp. on Polydora digitatum. Some areas had a 70% cover of Alcyonium digitatum where the surface was less even, with small gullies randomly orientated. Cliff faces had usual cover of mixed hydroids, bryozoans and tunicates. Bare broken vertical surfaces showed abundant occupied Hiatella arctica borings. Crossaster papposus was common, showing no obvious substrate prefernce. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Aplidium proliferum: Low flat platforms of chalk with dense Polydora spp. turf and large patches of Taonia atomaria, with occasional Delesseria, Hypoglossum hypoglossoides, Plocamium cartilagineum and Audouinella spp. Also sand with Sabellaria spinulosa. There was a kelp forest with a dense understorey of foliose algae. Polydora spp. in silt; Urticina felina in the crevices formed by sculpturing. Vertical faces were dominated by bryozoans (plus some sponges and Aplidium punctum), the ledges again were dominated by Polydora spp. Further offshore flat reefs, with the fauna gradually becoming similar to that of the offshore dive sites. Few reds, but Flustra foliacea appears and bryozoan turf gets thicker. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Flat topped platform dissected by surge gullies generally about 600 mm deep and 1.5 m wide. Flat areas dominated by Flustra foliacea, bryozoan turf, Polyclinum aurantium and Cliona spp. The shallow sided gullies had Alcyonium digitatum common on the top edges, otherwise similar cover to the flat areas. Rounded gully bottom - with no life and clean of sediment. NOTE: Substratum % data are convertedt from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Aplidium proliferum: Generally flat bedrock plain, with little vertical growth. Wide bedrock terraces apparently flat but infact gently shelving. Vertical faces up to 500 cm high facing east-north east, the width of the terraces about 5 m. Upper surfaces fairly bare with many small holes but no sign of live animals, mostly sediment filled. All colonies in upper surfaces small i.e. Flustra foliacea, Tubularia spp. and Alcyonium digitatum. The vertical surfaces swere extensively bored but again no live animals. There were many brittle stars in the holes and a rich faunal turf including many large hydroids, Diphasia pinnata and smaller species. Halecium spp. and Bugula spp., and a few Alcyonium digitatum and Sagartia spp. Small crabs in amonst the turf and larger crabs were seen in the crevices. No algae at all. There were small rounded ridges in the rock platform with no apparently uniformity of direction and no particular variety of species although wide gully bottom betweeen ridges some times bare and current swept. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Light algal cover, almost no kelps and the few specimens present were only on the top most points. Foliose red algal cover very thin - predominantly Delesseria sanguinea. Main cover was small bryozoan turf interspersed with banks of Flustra foliacea and small colonies of Alcyonium digitatum. Some areas with tunicate cover especially Polyclinum aurantium. No sponges recorded except for Cliona spp. Turf difficult to distinguish much, but Halecium spp. and Bugula plumosa prominent, also Clavelina lepadiformis. Vertical surfaces silty - less colonisation. On low lying bedrock there were often areas densely packed with a >cm thick mat of tubicolous amphipods. Sometimes uninterrupted; or with algae/small hydroids showing through. Very silty. Urticina spp. protruding in places. Ahnfeltia plicata: Broken rock platform consisting of lumps (of rock) >1 m wide, flat topped, with gullies and channels betoween. The channels were up to 0.5 m deep. In the base of channel, on the bedrock were pebbles, fine gravel and silt. A layer of flocculent silt was trapped over the whole surface of the rock. No seaweeds were seen and the rock was dominated by patches of Flustra foliacea and other bryozoans. Alcyonium digitatum and Asterias rubens were abundant. A few other bryozoans and hydroids were found but surveying was difficult due to extremely poor visibility. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Horizontal surfaces of large boulders and bedrock. Irregular bedrock with shallow gullies in kelp forest. Understorey of red weeds e.g. Delessaria sanguinea and large patches of Polyclinum aurantium. This soon gave way to more broken ground with deeper gullies and vertical edges of bedrock with fewer red weeds and a very rich animal turf. All species recorded for the area were present with no obviuos habitat preference. Occasional flat topped plateux, about 1 - 2 m above the general bottom and had sparse kelp cover with a more varied unerstorey of red weeds and faunal turf. Undercut edges had numerous crabs and a few lobsters and also one leopard spotted goby. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Rocky ridges with steep faces and terraces of about 2 m in height. Running 30 - 210 degrees, the ridges are not continuous but all with steeper and deeper faces facing ESE. No unusual species present except Echinus esculentus only rarely seen south of the head. An anchor was seen and there were many Cyanea spp. jellyfish seen in mid-water NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Kelp forest at 1.3 m acd. Laminaria hyperborea common with a carpet of red algae underneath. Reds mainy Polysiphonia, species again mainly covered with Modiolus modiolus, Furcellaria lumbricalis found in areas where there was much sand accumulated. In general the rock was covered with a thin film of coarse sand. The kelp stipes, were covered with Palmaria palmata (common) along with Polysiphonia sp., Ceramium rubrum, Membranoptera alata and encrusting algae. The vertical surfaces supported algae towards the top but faunal turf dominated 75% of the cover. Amongst this turf a few filamentous weeds were found. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

The vertical surfaces supported algae towards the top but faunal turf dominated 75% of the cover. Amongst this turf a few filamentous weeds were found. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Uppermost parts with a few kelp plants and some red algae, especialy Phyllophora spp., Schottera nicaeensis and Rhodymenia holmsii at the bases of the outcrops. The vertical surfaces were dominated by a faunal turf. Crisia spp. were abundant and covered substantial areas. Polyclinum spp. was also abundant. Aplidium punctum was noticeably more common than on horizontal surfaces . Aplidium proliferum was also common. Myxilla incrustans was also conspicuous in places. Bugula spp. present (especially Bugula turbinata). Few hydroid varieties but Alcyonium digitatum rare at this depth/site. Numerous Ophiothrix fragilis hidden within the topso f the crevices. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Rough ground of chalk bedrock plain with boulders, pebbles and cobbles. Current swept and the greatest cover was by red foliose and filamentous algae (e.g. Plocamium cartilagineum). These grew on the tops of smaller cobbles and boulders. Some of the larger boulders in particular were animal dominated. A faunal turf varying from clumps of Flustra foliacea, to growths of Alcyonidium spp. in various forms ranging from long, thin to considerably branched. Botryllus schlosseri also very common, either growing as a crust or as free lobes. Bryozoan turf - especially Bugula spp. Hydroids included Halecium spp. Clavelina lepadiformis also common. Very few sponges (except Leucosolenia) or anemones (except Urticina in sheltered pockets ). Loose, mobile pebbles, were widespread, remaining mostly uncolonised except for Pomatoceros spp. and filamentous algae - small growths. Few fish - no wrasse or cod family. Plaice were most conspicuous and a few others associated in holes. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.***

Ahnfeltia plicata: Small rocks were covered with algae, mixed with some Halecium spp. Flatter rocks were covered with Polydora spp. and silt. Clavelina lepadiformis forms many small clumps on many small rocks. There were pebbles with Pomatoceros spp. Sand and gravel was colonised with Sabella spp. and Lanice conchilega (but not very many). Boulders have more bryozoa (Flustra foliacea and small tufts of Bicellaria ciliata, Crisia spp. and Bugula spp.) than red algae. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.
Aplidium proliferum: Horizontal surfaces of large boulders and bedrock. Irregular bedrock with shallow gullies in kelp forest. Understorey of red weeds e.g. Delessaria sanguinea and large patches of Polyclinum aurantium. This soon gave way to more broken ground with deeper gullies and vertical edges of bedrock with fewer red weeds and a very rich animal turf. All species recorded for the area were present with no obviuos habitat preference. Occasional flat topped plateux, about 1 - 2 m above the general bottom and had sparse kelp cover with a more varied understorey of red weeds and faunal turf. Undercut edges had numerous crabs and a few lobsters and also one leopard spotted goby. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Stands of Laminaria hyperborea (common). The rock surface between were at least 50% covered by red algae. Sessile animals pernhaps 20%. The rest more or less bare with fine silt. The most common foliose algae was Plocamium cartilagineum. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Bedrock platform with some small channels. In one place a peice of Lithothamnion spp. had become attached to the animal turf. This was the only attached alga seen. The bedrock was slighty terraced - overall forming a bedrock plain. The bryozoan turf trapped silt. Archidistoma aggregatum was found to be frequent forming a more spongy turf in places. Ahnfeltia plicata: Generally flat bedrock plain, with little vertical growth. Wide bedrock terraces apparently flat but infact gently shelving. Vertical faces up to 500 cm high facing east-north east, the width of teh terraces about 5 m. Upper surfaces fairly bare with many small holes but no sign of live animals, mostly sediment filled. All colonies in upper surfaces small i.e. Flustra foliacea, Tubularia spp. and Alcyonium digitatum. The vertical surfaces swere extensively bored but again no live animals. There were many brittle stars in the holes and a rich faunal turf including many large hydroids, Diphasia pinnata and smaller species. Halecium spp. and Bugula spp., and a few Alcyonium digitatum and Sagartia spp. Small crabs in amonst the turf and larger crabs were seen in the crevices. No algae at all. There were small rounded ridges in the rock platform with no apparently uniformity of direction and no particular variety of species although wide gully bottom betweeen ridges some times bare and current swept. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: The whole site appeared to be one large single habitat, despite the small pockets of sediment as these did not appear to be colonised. Unstable pebbles also remained uncolonised, but most of the rocks were large enough to support a fairly dense turf of faunal species, especially the moderate sized boulders. Several species were conspicuous and common including Clavelina lepadiformis in lumps and masses, Alcyonidium spp. (single pieces, widespread especially on tops of rocks), Flustra foliacea (tops/sdies) of the rocks. Amongst the hydroids Halecium spp. was obvious, and the larger stouter Abietenaria spp. Bryozoans were abundant often dominating the boulder surfaces. Apart from Flustra foliacea the species involved were Bugula flabellata and Bugula plumosa. The small blobby tunicate Archidistoma abundant. Other species seen sporadically included the boring phase of Cliona spp., especially on low, small boulders, one or 2 clumps of Alcyonium digitatum. Rarely Sabella spp., protruding from sides of small boulders or silty sand pockets. Urticina felina was occasionally seen, on the bedrock boulder floor. Amongst mobile fauna - the commonest were young Asterias rubens, Hyas spp. and several Cancer pagurus and Pleuronectes spp. Otherwise few fish, just Callionymus lyra. Crossaster seen, and several Janolus were seen feeding on Bugula spp. Algae virtually non existant - just a few unidentified reds. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Bedrock with widely separated terraces and much rock debris. The horizontal surfaces were densely fringed with Flustra foliacea (30 - 60 cm from the edges of outcrops and terraces). The other horizontal areas had a more mixed turf of Crisia spp., Leucosolenia spp. and Polyclinum aurantium. Vertical and more steeply sloping faces similar but generally more Bugula spp., Clavelina lepadiformis and Diphasia spp. Areas of freshly broken rock often but not always, exposed piddocks and had numerous crabs Cancer pagurus and a few squat lobsters Galathea strigosa. Faunal turf generally less dense than in other sites dived in this region, with areas of uncolonised, or thinly colonised rock, usually with boring Cliona spp. visible. Some smaller unstable stones covered with Pomatoceros spp. and prawns underneath. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted fro mthe original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Small rocks were covered with algae, mixed with some Halecium spp. Flatter rocks were covered with Polydora spp. and silt. Clavelina lepadiformis forms many small clumps on many small rocks. There were pebbles with Pomatoceros spp. Sand and gravel was colonised with Sabella spp. and Lanice conchilega (but not very many). Aplidium proliferum: Kelp forest at 1.3 m acd. Laminaria hyperborea common with a carpet of red algae underneath. Reds mainly Polysiphonia, species again mainly covered with Modiolus modiolus, Furcellaria lumbricalis found in areas where there was much sand accumulated. In general the rock was covered with a thin film of coarse sand. The kelp stipes, were covered with Palmaria palmata (common) along with Polysiphonia sp., Ceramium rubrum, Membranoptera alata and encrusting algae. The vertical surfaces supported algae towards the top but faunal turf dominated 75% of the cover. Amongst this turf a few filamentous weeds were found. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: The whole site appeared to be one large single habitat, despite the small pckets of sediment as theses did not appear to be colonised. Unstable pebbles also remained uncolonised, but most of the rocks were large enough to support a fairly dense turf of faunal species, especially the moderate sized boulders. Several species were conspicuous and common includig Clavelina lepadiformis in lumps and masses, Alcyonidium spp. (single pieces, widespread especially on tops of rocks), Flustra foliacea on (tops/sides) of the rocks. Amongst the hydroids Halecium spp. was obvious, and the larger stouter Abietenaria spp. Bryozoans were abundant often dominating the boulder surfaces. Apart from Flustra foliacea the soecies involved were Bugula flabellata and Bugula plumosa. The small blobby tunicate Archidistoma aggregatum abundant. Other species seen sporadically included the boring phase of Cliona spp., especially on low, small boulders, one or two clumps of Alcyonium digitatum. Rarely Sabella spp., protruding from sides oef small boulders or silty sand pockets. Urticina felina was occasionally seen, on the bedrock boulder floor. Amongst mobile fauna - the commonest were young Asterias rubens, Hyas spp. and several Cancer pagurus and Pleuronectes spp. Otherwise few fish, just Callionymus lyra. Crossaster papposus seen, and sevral Janolus cristatus were seen feeding on Bugula spp. Algae virtually non existant - just a few unidentified reds.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Large and small cobbles with an occasional boulder covered in a thin layer of silt that became thick in places. The occasional large and small boulders had kelp on the tops. Aplidium punctum was noticeably more common than on horizontal surfaces . Aplidium proliferum was also common. Myxilla incrustans was also conspicuous in places. Bugula spp. present (especially Bugula turbinata). Few hydroid varieties but Alcyonium digitatum rare at this depth/site. Numerous Ophiothrix fragilais hidden within the tops of the crevices. Ahnfeltia plicata: Wave cut platform in chalk with low ledges. Main feature of this habitat was the rich faunal turf on cobbles and boulders. Good diversity and % cover of bryozoans (esp. Flustra foliacea, Bugula spp. and Bicellaria spp.), hydroids (Halecium ciliata and Campanularia verticillatus) and tunicates (including Clavelina lepadiformis, Polyclinum aurautium, Botryllus sclosseri, Botrylloides leachi, with lesser quantities of Aplidium spp. and Sidnyum spp.). A few sponges including Leucosolenia spp. on rocks, Cliona spp. boring into rocks and Polydora spp. evident in bare patches of bedrock. Alcyonidium diaphanum was abundant on most substrates especilly on bare rock. Ahnfeltia plicata: Small rocks were covered with algae, mixed with some Halecium spp. Flatter rocks were covered with Polydora spp. and silt. Boulders have more bryozoa (Flustra foliacea and small tufts of Bicellaria ciliata, Crisia spp. and Bugula spp.) than red algae. Aplidium proliferum: Some areas largely uncolonised (possibly due to scour/mobility) some with a similar turf to habitats 1 and 2. On the colonised areas Alcyonium digitatum was common, - with many Acanthodoris pilosa feeding and laying eggs on them. Animals present here but not on the upper. These included Haliclona oculata and the sunstar Crossaster papposus. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Aplidium proliferum: Rock platform, below reef face with polychaete tubes, mainly Sabellaria spinulosa and silt. Reef face a mixture of bryozoan /hydroid turf either Alcyonium digitatum or Flustra foliacea in dense stands. Bryozoan turf slightly different with Eucratea loricata and Vesicularia spinosa joining Scrupocellaria spp. and Bugula spp. Small stones covered with Cliona spp. which appears to prefer this to the bedrock. The stones were also covered with Pomatoceros and the encrusting bryozoan Escharella immersa. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Larger, more stable boulders and cobbles. These were more densely colonised by growths of foliose and filamentous red and brown algae including Laminaria saccharina. On the sides of these cobbles there ere turf species including Clavelina lepadiformis, Leucosolenia spp., Flustra foliacea and Bugula spp. Underneath were prawns and Ophiothrix fraglis. Also obsereved were a few large detached Botryllus schlosseri. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Aplidium proliferum: Generally flat bedrock plain, with little vertical growth. Wide bedrock terraces apparently flat but infact gently shelving. Vertical faces up to 500 cm high facing east-north east, the width of the terraces about 5 m. Upper surfaces fairly bare with many small holes but no sign of live animals, mostly sediment filled. All colonies in upper surfaces small i.e. Flustra foliacea, Tubularia spp. and Alcyonium digitatum. The vertical surfaces swere extensively bored but again no live animals. There were many brittle stars in the holes and a rich faunal turf including many large hydroids, Diphasia pinnata and smaller species. Halecium spp. and Bugula spp., and a few Alcyonium digitatum and Sagartia spp. Small crabs in amonst the turf and larger crabs were seen in the crevices. No algae at all. There were small rounded ridges in the rock platform with no apparently uniformity of direction and no particular variety of species although wide gully bottom betweeen ridges some times bare and current swept. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Uppermost parts with a few kelp plants and some red lagae, especialy Phyllophora spp., Schottera nicaeensis and Rhodymenia holmsii at the bases of the outcrops. The vertical surfaces were dominated by a faunal turf. Crisia spp. were abundant and covered substantial areas. Polyclinum spp. was also abundant. Aplidium punctum was noticeably more common than on horizontal surfaces . Aplidium proliferum was also common. Myxilla incrustans was also conspicuous in places. Bugula spp. present (especially Bugula turbinata). Few hydroid varieties but Alcyonium digitatum rare at this depth/site. Numerous Ophiothrix fragilis hidden within the tops of the crevices. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Horizontal bedrock surface with a dense cover of Flustra foliacea grading to equally dense cover of Polydora spp. tubes at the base of the next ledge; lots of Asterias rubens and a fewer Henricia spp. on Polydora digitatum. Some areas had a 70% cover of Alcyonium digitatum where the surface was less even, with samll gullies randomly orientated. Cliff faces had usual cover of mixed hydroids, bryozoans and tunicates. Bare broken vertical surfaces showed abundant occupied Hiatella arctica borings. Crossaster papposus was common, showing no obvious substrate prefernce. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Only sparse algal cover at this site - Plocamium cartilagineum, Delessaria sanguinea, Taonia atomaria and others. Laminaria hyperborea - one single plant, small and on top of a boulder. One of the most prominant animals was Flustra foliacea, often almost completely covering the sides of boulders. The other was Polyclinum aurantium, occuring in dense mats in places. There was in many places a well developed faunal turf especially common were Bugula spp., Setularia spp., Clavelina lepadiformis and also hydroids including Halecium spp., Archidistoma aggregatum also common in places - mat like straggly Alcyonidium spp. especially on the boulder tops. The flat bedrock was noticeably less well colonised - considerably scouring must occur, but spionid wowrms in evidence; small silty tubes strewn on the rock surface. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Broken rock platform consisting of lumps (of rock) >1 m wide, flat topped, with gullies and channels between. The channels were up to 0.5 m deep. In the base of channel, on the bedrock were pebbles, fine gravel and silt. A layer of flocculent silt was trapped over the whole surface of the rock. No seaweeds were seen and the rock was dminated by patches of Flustra foliacea and other bryozoans. Alcyonium digitatum and Asterias rubens were abundant. A few other bryozoans and hydroids were found but surveying was difficult due to extremely poor visibility. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Boulders have more bryozoa (Flustra foliacea and small tufts of Bicellaria ciliata, Crisia spp. and Bugula spp.) than red algae. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.
Aplidium proliferum: Bryozoan turf dominated - mostly small species especially Crisia spp. and also Bugula plumosa & Bugula flabellata were common. Hydroids included Diphasia spp. and Nemertesia spp. Good variety of mobile life. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Aplidium proliferum: A horizontal cleft typically 1.5 - 2 m above the sea bed and up to 2 m deep into the rock. Sometimes flat ledge forming the lower side of the cleft with silty surface and Urticina felina and Haliclona oculata on it with some Alcyonidium spp. Outer top of the cleft sponge dominated with some Tubularia spp. The cleft was rich in mobile fauna - squat lobster, lobster, edible crabs - many of a large size. Also a leopard spotted goby, and a Yarrels Blenny and a good sized cod seen going into one hole. The lower vertical surface had a thin turf layer, many Hiatella spp., the lowest half metre was scoured clean - often with a rounded profile. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Bedrock with widely separated terraces and much rock debris. The horizontal surfaces were densely fringed with Flustra foliacea (30 - 60 cm from the edges of outcrops and terraces). The other horizontal areas had a more mixed turf of Crisia spp., Leucosolenia spp. and Polyclinum aurantium. Vertical and more steeply sloping faces similar but generally more Bugula spp., Clavelina lepadiformis and Diphasia pp. Areas of freshly broken rock often but not always, exposed piddocks and had numerous crabs Cancer pagurus and a few squat lobsters Galathea strigosa. Faunal turf generally less dense than in other sites dived in this region, with areas of uncolonised, or thinly colonised rock, usually with boring Cliona spp. visible. Some smaller unstable stones covered with Pomatoceros spp. and prawns underneath. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Uppermost parts with a few kelp plants and some red algae, especialy Phyllophora spp., Schottera nicaeensis and Rhodymenia holmsii at the bases of the outcrops. The vertical surfaces were dominated by a faunal turf. Crisia spp. were abundant and covered substantial areas. Polyclinum spp. was also abundant. Aplidium punctum was noticeably mroe common than on horizontal surfaces . Aplidium proliferum was also common. Myxilla incrustans was also conspicuous in places. Bugula spp. present (especially Bugula turbinata). Few hydroid varieties but Alcyonium digitatum rare at this depth/site. Numerous Ophiothrix fragilis hidden within the tops of the crevices. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Aplidium proliferum: Large and small cobbles with an occasional boulder covered in a thin layer of silt that became thick in places. The occasional large and small boulders had kelp on the tops. Species included Calliblepharis ciliata, Taonia atomaria, Ceramium rubrum and Phyllophora pseudoceranoides. Faunal species included Flustra foliacea, Henricia spp., Asterias rubens, Carcinus maenas and Cancer pagurus. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Aplidium proliferum: Mixture of cobbles, boulders and pebbles with fauna typical for the area but overlain to a greater or lesser extent by coarse sand which mostly buried all but the largest cobbles and boulders - hence available hard substrate was limited and presumably affected by scour. Turf communities consisted of Flustra foliacea, Bicellaria ciliata, Halecium spp., Bugula spp., Campanularia spp. with fewer tunicates (only Clavelina lepadiformis, Botryllus schlosseri and Botrylloides leachi forming a significant part ) than elsewhere (no Polyclinum aurantium or Archidistoma aggregatum). Plenty of crabs (Carcinus maenas) and Asterias rubens in evidence with sevral large Crossaster papposus. Urticina felina common poking up through the sand. Very few weeds present mostly as small patches. A few unpleasant objects floated past or were found rotting on the seabed. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Broken rock platform consisting of lumps (of rock) >1 m wide, flat topped, with gullies and channels between. The channels were up to 0.5 m deep. In the base of channel, on the bedrock were pebbles, fine gravel and silt. A layer of flocculent silt was trapped over the whole surface of the rock. No seaweeds were seen and the rock was dominated by patches of Flustra foliacea and other bryozoans. Alcyonium digitatum and Asterias rubens were abuondant. A few other bryozoans and hydroids were found but surveying was difficult due to extremely poor visibility. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Flat bedrock plain, at inner edge featureless with no crevices/ cracks etc. towards the boundary of habitat 3 becoming fissured with irregular cracks generally about 100 mm deep. There was a thin covering f coarse sand in parts and foliose algae ( Polysiphonia nigra and Plocamium cartilagineum). Attached animal cover was very limited, Polyclinum aurantium again was again very common as was Haliclona oculata and Alcyonidium diaphanum and Urticina felina was in crevices. There was a slight rise to habitat 3. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Horizontal bedrock surface with a dense cover of Flustra foliacea grading to equally dense cover of Polydora spp. tubes at the base of the next ledge; lots of Asterias rubens and a fewer Henricia spp. on Polydora digitatum. Some areas had a 70% cover of Alcyonium digitatum where the surface was less even, with small gullies randomly orientated. Cliff faces had usual cover of mixed hydroids, bryozoans and tunicates. Bare broken vertical surfaces showed abundant occupied Hiatella arctica borings. Crossaster papposus was common, showing no obvious substrate prefernce. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 13 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Wave cut platform in chalk with low ledges.d Main feature of this habitat was the rich faunal turf on cobbles and boulders. Good diversity and % cover of bryozoans (esp. Flustra foliacea, Bugula spp. and Bicellaria spp.), hydroids (Halecium ciliata and Campanularia verticillatus) and tunicates (including Clavelina lepadiformis, Polyclinum aurautium, Botryllus sclosseri, Botrylloides leachi, with lesser quantities of Aplidium spp. and Sidnyum spp.). A few sponges including Leucosolenia spp. on rocks, Cliona spp. boring into rocks and Polydora spp. evident in bare patches of bedrock. Alcyonidium diaphanum was abundant on most substrates especilly on bare rock. Mobile fauna was mainly Asterias rubens and crabs (Cancer pagurus was very common) and suprisingly Carcinus maenas (2 specimens were seen). NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Other species included the burrowing form of Cliona spp., juevenile Henricia spp., Asterias rubens, Aplidium proliferatum, Sidnyum turbinatum, Clavelina lepadiformis and Botryllus schlosseri. Ahnfeltia plicata: Tops of outcrops had tatty kelp, the plants smaller than 1 m were overgrown with Obelia spp. and Membranipora mambranacea. The stipes were encrusted with Electra pilosa and Halichondria panicea and red algae. Areas between the kelp with a range of turf species and encrusting species. There were large patches of Myxilla incrustans and Halichondria panicea. Other species included Clavelina loepadiformis, Aplidium spp. and Botrylus schlosseri common smaller species included Crisia spp. and Hiatella arctica. Noticeably fewer Alcyonium digitatum and Flustra foliacea than in deeper sites and more sponges. The sides of the outcrops had fewer algae than elsewhere. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Aplidium proliferum: Rock platform, below reef face with polychaete tubes, mainly Sabellaria spinulosa and silt. Aplidium proliferum: Bedrock with widely separated terraces and much rock debris. The horizontal surfaces were densely fringed with Flustra foliacea (30 - 60 cm from the edges of outcrops and terraces). The other horizontal areas had a more mixed turf of Crisia spp., Leucosolenia spp. and Polyclinum aurantium. Vertical and more steeply sloping faces similar but generally more Bugula spp., Clavelina lepadiformis and Diphasia spp. Areas of freshly broken rock often but not always, exposed piddocks and had numerous crabs Cancer pagurus and a few squat lobsters Galathea strigosa. Faunal turf generally less dense than in other sites dived in this region, with areas of uncolonised, or thinly colonised rock, usually with boring Cliona spp. visible. Some smaller unstable stones covered with Pomatoceros spp. and prawns underneath. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Bedrock surfaces formed from uvarious gullies, bedrock and small boulders. Occasional large boulders with sharply undercut edges in depressions in the bedrock. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Aplidium proliferum: Bedrock platform arranged in terraces. The dominant attached animal was Alcyonium digitatum quite deeply covering the horizontal and vertical surfaces. Also common were Flustra foliacea, Bugula flabellata and Alcyonidium spp. and a dense turf that seemed to be dominated by Crisia eburnea. Tubularia spp. stalks were in evidence; also very noticeable were the large numbers of amphipod whips, especially protruding from the vertical faces. Also protruding from vertical rocks - crevice were arms of Ophiothrix fragilis in large numbers. On the horizontal tops, but especially on flatter, low profile bedrock Cliona celata (boring phase) was common, otherwise there were very few sponges. Diphasia spp. was common in clumps (like Halecium spp. formed on the south side of the penninsula). Anemones very rare; sponges - ditto (except Cliona spp. and some Leucosolenia spp.). A good variety of crustaceans. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: The dominant ecnrusting species was Halichondria panicea in extensive sheets. Electra pilosa was found towards the base of the stipes. The common algae inculded Phycodrys rubens, Membranoptera alata, Plocamium cartilagineum, and Ceramium spp.(Sub-habitat of 145.042.001) NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Broken rock platform consisting of lumps (of rock) >1 m wide, flat topped, with gullies and channels between. dThe channels were up to 0.5 m deep. In the base of channel, on the bedrock were pebbles, fine gravel and silt. A layer of flocculent silt was trapped over the whole surface of the rock. No seaweeds were seen and the rock was dominated by patches of Flustra foliacea and other bryozoans. Alcyonium digitatum and Asterias rubens were abundant. A few other bryozoans and hydroids were found but surveying was difficult due to extremely poor visibility. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Irregular bedrock with rounded tops, shallow gullies and fissures and crevices. No obvious orientation of features. Kelp forest dominated. (Laminaria hyperborea) with stipes about 1.5 m long. The depth was about 3.5 m bcd. Understorey of foliose red algae on all upper surfaces (not exceeding 300 mm ) the dominant animal was Polyclinum aurantium with much smaller numbers of other tunicates, small sponges and other hydroids. There were few bryozoans in amongst this turf. Kelp stipes were extensively colonised by Halichondria panicea and a number of spider crabs Hyas araneus observed clutching the stipes. Most fronds were uncolonised though some were covered with Membranipora membranacea. here was a distinct drop of 1 m to habitat 2. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Only sparse algal cover at this site - Plocamium cartilagineum, Delessaria sanguinea, Taonia atomaria and others. Laminaria hyperborea - one single plant, small and on top of a boulder. One of the most prominant animals was Flustra foliacea, often almost completely covering the sides of boulders. The other was Polyclinum aurantium, occuring in dense mats in places. There was in many places a well developed faunal turf especially common were Bugula spp., Setularia spp., Clavelina lepadiformis and also hydroids including Halecium spp., Archidistoma aggregatum also common in places - mat like straggly Alcyonidium spp. especially on the boulder tops. The flat bedrock was noticeably less well colonised - considerably scouring munst occur, but spionid worms in evidence; small silty tubes strewn on the rock surface. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Horizontal surfaces of large boulders and bedrock. Irregular bedrock with shallow gullies in kelp forest. Understorey of red weeds e.g. Delessaria sanguinea and large patches of Polyclinum aurantium. This soon gave way to more broken ground with deeper gullies and vertical edges of bedrock with fewer red weeds and a very rich animal turf. All species recorded for the area were present with no obviuos habitat preference. Occasional flat topped plateux, about 1 - 2 m above the general bottom and had sparse kelp cover with a more varied understorey of red weeds and faunal turf. Undercut edges had numeruos crabs and a few lobsters and also one leopard spotted goby. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Bedrock plain with small channels 10 - 40 cm deep running through with a small amount of gravel/cobbles in the bottom. Rock covered in a bryozoan turf consisting of a mixture of species including Crisia spp., Bugula turbinata and Eucratea loricata. Alcyonium digitatum was abundant. Other species included the burrowing form of Cliona spp., juevenile Henricia spp., Asterias rubens, Aplidium proliferatum, Sidnyum turbinatum, Clavelina lepadiformis and Botryllus schlosseri. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.
Aplidium proliferum: The main difference at this site was the abundance of Alcyonium digitatum - greater than in other areas; possibly due to the greater stability of rocks here. Some Polyclinum aurantium and condsiderable patches of Archidistoma aggregatum - as at other sites. Flustra foliacea also abundant on current swept surfaces. No sponges recorded except for Cliona spp. Turf difficult to distinguish much, but Halecium spp. and Bugula plumosa prominent, also Clavelina lepadiformis. Vertical surfaces silty - less colonisation. On low lying bedrock there were often areas densely packed with a >cm thick mat of tubicolous amphipods. Sometimes uninterrupted; or with algae/small hydroids showing through. Very silty. Urticina spp. protruding in places. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Only sparse algal cover at this site - Plocamium cartilagineum, Delessaria sanguinea, Taonia atomaria and others. Laminaria hyperborea - one single plant, small and on top of a boulder. One of the most prominant animals was Flustra foliacea, often almost completely covering the sides of boulders. The other was Polyclinum aurantium, occuring in dense mats in places. There was in many places a well developed faunal turf especially common were Bugula spp., Setularia spp., Clavelina lepadiformis and also hydroids including Halecium spp., Archidistoma aggregatum also common in place s- mat like straggly Alcyonidium spp. especially on the boulder tops. The flat bedrock was noticeably less well colonised - considerably scouring must occur, but spionid worms in evidence; small silty tubes strewn on the rock surface. Aplidium proliferum: Rock platform, below reef face with polychaete tubes, mainly Sabellaria spinulosa and silt. Reef face a mixture of bryozoan /hydroid turf either Alcyonium digitatum or Flustra foliacea in dense stands. Bryozoan turf slightly different with Eucratea loricata and Vesicularia spinosa joining Scrupocellaria spp. and Bugula spp. Small stones covered with Cliona spp. which appears to prefer this to the bedrock. The stones were also covered with Pomatoceros and the encrusting bryozoan Escharella immersa. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: The whole site appeared to be one large single habitat, despite the small pockets of sediment as these did not appear to be colonised. Unstable pebbles also remained uncolonised, but most of the rocks were large enough to support a fairly dense turf of faunal species, especially the moderate sized boulders. Several species were conspicuous and common including Clavelina lepadiformis in lumps and masses, Alcyonidium spp. (single pieces, widespread especially on tops of rocks), Flustra foliacea (tops/sides) of the rocks. Amongst the hydroids Halecium spp. was obvious, and the larger stouter Abietenaria spp. Bryozoans were abundant often dominating the boulder surfaces. Apart from Flustra foliacea the species involved were Bugula flabellata and Bugula plumosa. The small blobby tunicate Archidistoma abundant. Other species seen sporadically included the boring phase of Cliona spp., especially on low, small boulders, one or 2 clumps of Alcyonium digitatum. Rarely Sabella spp., protruding from sides of small boulders or silty sand pockets. Urticina felina wvas occasionally seen, on the bedrock boulder floor. Amongst mobile fauna - the commonest were young Asterias rubens, Hyas spp. and several Cancer pagurus and Pleuronectes spp. Otherwise few fish, just Callionymus lyra. Crossaster seen, and several Janolus were seen feeding on Bugula spp. Algae virtually non existant - just a few unidentified reds. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Aplidium proliferum: Rough ground of chalk bedrock plain with boulders, pebbles and cobbles. Current swept and the greatest cover was by red foliose and filamentous algae (e.g. Plocamium cartilagineum). These grew on the tops of smaller cobbles and boulders. Some of the larger boulders in particular were animal dominated. A faunal turf varying from clumps of Flustra foliacea, to growths of Alcyonidium spp. in various forms ranging from long, thin to considerably branched. Botryllus schlosseri also very common, either growing as a crust or as free lobes. Bryozoan turf - especially Bugula spp. Hydroids included Halecium spp. Clavelina lepadiformis also common. Very few sponges (except Leucosolenia) or anemones (except Urticina in sheltered pockets ). Loose, mobile pebbles, were widespread, remaining mostly uncolonised except for Pomatoceros spp. and filamentous algae - small growths. Few fish - no wrasse or cod family. Plaice were most conspicuous and a few others associated in holes. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Bedrock ledges, outcrops and wide gullies which were algal dominated. Delesseria sanguinea frequent with decreasing depth. Ahnfeltia plicata: Thin kelp forest at 0.5 - 2.5 m with some red algae, which was not very profuse. Holdfasts generally small with Crisia spp. and Botryllus schlosseri being the main inhabitant. Other fauna on horizontal surfaces tended to be sparse and erratic, these species included Tubularia spp., Alcyonium digitatum and Botryllus schlosseri. The vertical components of the bedrock had a rich and varied animal turf with species including Bugula spp. and Clavelina lepadiformis. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.
Aplidium proliferum: Small rocks were covered with algae, mixed with some Halecium spp. Flatter rocks were covered with Polydora spp. and silt. Clavelina lepadiformis forms many small clumps on many small rocks. There were pebbles with Pomatoceros spp. Sand and gravel was colonised with Sabella spp. and Lanice conchilega (but not very many). Boulders have more bryozoa (Flustra foliacea and small tufts of Bicellaria ciliata, Crisia spp. and Bugula spp.) than red algae. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Larger, more stable boulders and cobbles. These were more densely colonised by growths of foliose and filamentous red and brown algae including Laminaria saccharina. On the sides of these cobbles there were turf species including Clavelina lepadiformis, Leucosolenia spp., Flustra foliacea and Bugula spp. Underneath were prawns and Ophiothrix fraglis. Also obsereved were a zfew large detached Botryllus schlosseri. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Broken rock platform consisting of lumps (of rock) >1 m wide, flat topped, with gullies and channels between. The channels were up to 0.5 m deep. In the base of channel, on the bedrock were pebbles, fine gravel and silt. A layer of flocculent silt was trapped over the whole surface of the rock. No seaweeds were seen and the rock was dominated by patches of Flustra foliacea and other bryozoans.Alcyonium digitatum and Asterias rubens were abundant. A few other bryozoans and hydroids were found but surveying was difficult due to extremely poor visibility. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Aplidium proliferum: Rough ground of chalk bedrock plain with boulders, pebbles and cobbles. Current swept and the greatest cover was by red foliose and filamentous algae (e.g. Plocamium cartilagineum). These grew on the tops of smaller cobbles and boulders. Some of the larger boulders in particular were animal dominated. A faunal turf varying from clumps of Flustra foliacea, to growths of Alcyonidium spp. in various forms ranging from long, thin to considerably branched. Botryllus schlosseri also very common, either growing as a crust or as free lobes. Bryozoan turf - especially Bugula spp. Hydroids included Halecium spp. Clavelina lepadiformis also common. Very few sponges (except Leucosolenia) or anemones (except Urticina in sheltered pockets ). Loose, mobile pebbles, were widespread, remaining mostly uncolonised except for Pomatoceros spp. and filamentous algae - small growths. Few fish - no wrasse or cod family. Plaice were most conspicuous and a few others associated in holes. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Wave cut platform in chalk with low ledges. Main feature of this habitat was the rich faunal turf on cobbles and boulders. Good diversity and % cover of bryozoans (esp. Flustra foliacea, Bugula spp. and Bicellaria spp.), hydroids (Halecium ciliata and Campanularia verticillatus) andl tunicates (including Clavelina lepadiformis, Polyclinum aurautium, Botryllus sclosseri, Botrylloides leachi, with lesser quantities of Aplidium spp. and Sidnyum spp.). A few sponges including Leucosolenia spp. on rocks, Cliona spp. boring into rocks and Polydora spp. evident in bare patches of bedrock. Alcyonidium diaphanum was abundant on most substrates especilly on bare rock. Mobile fauna was mainly Asterias rubens and crabs (Cancer pagurus was very common) and suprisingly Carcinus maenas (2 specimens were seen). NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Aplidium proliferum: Bedrock with widely separated terraces and much rock debris. The horizontal surfaces were densely fringed with Flustra foliacea (30 - 60 cm from the edges of outcrops and terraces). The other horizontal areas had a more mixed turf of Crisia spp., Leucosolenia spp. and Polyclinum aurantium. Vertical and more steeply sloping faces similar but generally more Bugula spp., Clavelina lepadiformis and Diphasia spp. Areas of freshly broken rock often but not always, exposed piddocks and had numerous crabs Cancer pagurus and a few squat lobsters Galathea strigosa. Faunal turf generally less dense than in other sites dived in this region, with areas of uncolonised, or thinly colonised rock, usually with boring Cliona spp. visible. Some smaller unstable stones covered with Pomatoceros spp. and prawns underneath. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Aplidium proliferum: Bedrock platform arranged in terraces. The dominant attached animal was Alcyonium digitatum quite deeply covering the horizontal and vertical surfaces. Also common were Flustra foliacea, Bugula flabellata and Alcyonidium spp. and a dense turf that seemed to be dominated by Crisia eburnea. Tubularia spp. stalks were in evidence; also very noticeable were the large numbers of amphipod whips, especially protruding from the vertical faces. Also protruding from vertical rocks - crevice were arms of Ophiothrix fragilis in large numbers. On the horizontal tops, but especially on flatter, low profile bedrock Cliona celata (boring phase) was common, otherwise there were very few sponges. Diphasia spp. was common in clumps (like Halecium spp. formed on the south side of the penninsula). Anemones very rare; sponges - ditto (except Cliona spp. and some Leucosolenia spp.). A good variety of crustaceans. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Aplidium proliferum: Rocky ridges with steep faces and terraces of about 2 m in height. Running 30 - 210 degrees, the ridges are not continuous but all with steeper and deeper faces facing ESE. Virtually no algae and steep faces dominated by mixed animal turf including a mixture of hydroids, bryozoans and tunicates as recorded at other sites in the area. No unusual species present except Echinus esculentus only rarely seen south of the head. An anchor was seen and there were many Cyanea spp. jellyfish seen in mid-water. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Aplidium proliferum: Mixture of cobbles, boulders and pebbles with fauna typical for the area but overlain to a greater or lesser extent by coarse sand which mostly buried all but the largest cobbles and boulders - hence available hard substrate was limited and presumably affected by scour. Turf communities consisted of Flustra foliacea, Bicellaria ciliata, Halecium spp., Bugula spp., Campanularia spp. with fewer tunicates (only Clavelina lepadiformis, Botryllus schlosseri and Botrylloides leachi forming a significant part ) than elsewhere (no Polyclinum aurantium or Archidistoma aggregatum). Plenty of crabs (Carcinus maenas) and Asterias rubens in evidence with sevral large Crossaster papposus. Urticina felina common poking up through the sand. Very few weeds present mostly as small patches. A few unpleasant objects floated past or were found rotting on the seabed. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Bedrock with widely separated terraces and much rock debris. The horizontal surfaces were densely fringed with Flustra foliacea (30 - 60 cm from the edges of outcrops and terraces). The other horizontal araes had a more mixed turf of Crisia spp., Leucosolenia spp. and Polyclinum aurantium. Vertical and more steeply sloping faces similar but generally more Bugula spp., Clavelina lepadiformis and Diphasia spp. Areas of freshly broken rock often but not always, exposed piddocks and had numerous crabs Cancer pagurus and a few squat lobsters Galathea strigosa. Faunal turf generally less dense than in other sites dived in this region, with areas of uncolonised, or thinly colonised rock, usually with boring Cliona spp. visible. Some smaller unstable stones covered with Pomatoceros spp. and prawns underneath. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Small rocks were covered with algae, mixed with some Halecium spp. Flatter rocks were covered with Polydora spp. and silt. Clavelina lepadiformis forms many small clumps on many small rocks. There were pebbles with Pomatoceros spp. Sand and gravel was colonised with Sabella spp. and Lanice conchilega (but not very many). Boulders have more bryozoa (Flustra foliacea and small tufts of Bicellaria ciliata, Crisia spp. and Bugula spp.) than red algae. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.
Aplidium proliferum: Kelp forest 2.9 m bcd, Laminaria hyperborea common on the reef tops being easy to swim through. Various Polysiphonia sp. were found to be common on the rocks along with Plocamium cartilagineum. The Polysiphonia spp. were found to be encrusted with Modiolus spp. Coarse sand was found as a thin covering of the rocks. The kelp stipes were covered with a variety of reds including Membranoptera alata. Phycodrys rubens, Plocamium cartilagineum and Ptilota plumosa. The rock faces had reds on the upper parts but the middle and bottoms were dominated by the animal fauna typical of the area, sponges, bryozoans and tunicates. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Aplidium proliferum: Generally flat bedrock plain, with little vertical growth. Wide bedrock terraces apparently flat but infact gently shelving. Vertical faces up to 500 cm high facing east-north east, the width of the terraces about 5 m. Upper surfaces fairly bare with many small holes but no sign of live animals, mostly sediment filled. All colonies in upper surfaces small i.e. Flustra foliacea, Tubularia spp. and Alcyonium digitatum. The vertical surfaces swere extensively bored but again no live animals. There were many brittle stars in the holes and a rich faunal turf including many large hydroids, Diphasia pinnata and smaller species. Halecium spp. and Bugula spp., and a few Alcyonium digitatum and Sagartia spp. Small crabs in amonst the turf and larger crabs were seen in the crevices. No algae at all. There were small rounded ridges in the rock platform with no apparently uniformity of direction and no particular variety of species although wide gully bottom betweeen ridges some times bare and current swept. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Large and small cobbles with an occasional boulder covered in a thin layer of silt that became thick in places. The occasional large and small boulders hadkelp on the tops. Flustra foliacea on the sides orientated to the current. Scrupocellaria spp., Botrylloides leachi and Bugula spp. were also present. Red algae were abundant all over the site. Species included Calliblepharis ciliata, Taonia atomaria, Ceramium rubrum and Phyllophora pseudoceranoides. Faunal species included Flustra foliacea, Henricia spp., Asterias rubens, Carcinus maenas and Cancer pagurus. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Aplidium proliferum: Bedrock platform with a few channels and pebbles in places. Rocks covered in flocculent silt. Patches of Flustra foliacea with Crisia eburnea associated with it. No seaweeds were recorded. Alcyonium digitatum were common and Hyas coarctatus and Inachus phalangium were frequently observed. One large Echinus esculentus was seen and a number of Crossaster papposus. Patches of largish pebbles were found. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Mixture of cobbles, boulders and pebbles with fauna ytpical for the area but overlain to a greater or lesser extent by coarse sand which mostly buried all but the largest cobbles and boulders - hence available hard substrate was limited and presumably affected by scour. Turf communities consisted of Flustra foliacea, Bicellaria ciliata, Halecium spp., Bugula spp., Campanularia spp. with fewer tunicates (only Clavelina lepadiformis, Botryllus schlosseri and Botrylloides leachi forming a significant part ) than elsewhere (no Polyclinum aurantium or Archidistoma aggregatum). Plenty of crabs (Carcinus maenas) and Asterias rubens in evidence with sevral large Crossaster papposus. Urticina felina common poking up through the sand. Very few weeds present mostly as small patches. A few unpleasant objects floated past or were found rotting on the seabed. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Rock outcrops up to 6 m rhigh. Sides are vertical, steep sloping or overhanging. Typically lowest half metre rounded and scoured clean by wave and cobble action. Rock strata horizontally bedded providing lots of deep clefts up to 2 m in and 30 cm high. Often runnuing continuously along considerable legnths of rock. Tops of rock, kelp forest with foliose red algal understorey. The upper vertical faces of rock were dominated by the foliose algae. Upper overhanging face had a rich faunal turf - unusually for the area based on sponges esp. Halichondria panicea. Rocks riddled with holes, many occupied by Hiatella arctica or Ophiothrix fragilis. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Flat topped platform dissected by surge gullies generally about 600 mm deep and 1.5 m wide. Rounded gully bottom - with no life and clean of sediment. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Rocky ridges with steep faces and terraces of about 2 m in height. No unusual species present except Echinus esculentus only rarely seen south of the head. An anchor was seen and there were many Cyanea spp. jellyfish seen in mid-water. Ahnfeltia plicata: Wave cut platform in chalk with low ledges. Main feature of this habitat was the rich faunal turf on cobbles and boulders. Good diversity and % cover of bryozoans (esp. Flustra foliacea, Bugula spp. and Bicellaria spp.), hydroids (Halecium ciliata and Campanularia verticillatus) and tunicates (including Clavelina lepadiformis, Polyclinum aurautium, Botryllus sclosseri, Botrylloides leachi, with lesser quantities of Aplidium spp. and Sidnyum spp.). A few sponges including Leucosolenia spp. on rocks, Cliona spp. boring into rocks and Polydora spp. evident in bare patches of bedrock. Alcyonidium diaphanum was abundant on most substrates especilly on bare rock. Mobile fauna was mainly Asteriasr ubens and crabs (Cancer pagurus was very common) and suprisingly Carcinus maenas (2 specimens were seen). NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Horizontal bedrock surface with a dense cover of Flustra foliacea grading to equally dense cover of Polydora spp. tubes at the base of the next ledge; lots of Asterias rubens and a fewer Henricia spp. on Polydora digitatum. Some areas had a 70% cover of Alcyonium digitatum where the surface was less even, with small gullies randomly orientated. Cliff faces had usual cover of mixed hydroids, bryozoans and tunicates. Bare broken vertical surfaces showed abundant occupied Hiatella arctica borings. Crossaster papposus was common, showuing no obvious substrate prefernce. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Aplidium proliferum: Bedrock platform arranged in terraces. The dominant attached animal was Alcyonium digitatum quite deeply covering the horizontal and vertical surfaces. Also common were Flustra foliacea, Bugula flabellata and Alcyonidium spp. and a dense turf that seemed to be dominated by Crisia eburnea. Tubularia spp. stalks were in evidence; also very noticeable were the large numbers of amphipod whips, especially protruding from the vertical faces. Also protruding from vertical rocks - crevice were arms of Ophiothrix fragilis in large numbers. On the horizontal tops, but especially on flatter, low profile bedrock Cliona celata (boring phase) was common, otherwise there were very few sponges. Diphasia spp. was common in clumps (like Halecium spp. formed on the south side of the penninsula). Anemones very rare; sponges - ditto (except Cliona spp. and some Leucosolenia spp.). A good variety of crustaceans. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Rough ground of chalk bedrock plain with boulders, pebbles and cobbles. Current swept and the greatest cover was by red foliose and filamentous algae (e.g. Plocamium cartilagineum). These grew on the tops of smaller cobbles and boulders. Some of the larger boulders in particular were animal dominated. A faunal turf varying from clumps of Flustra foliacea, to growths of Alcyonidium spp. in various forms ranging from long, thin to considerably branched. Botryllus schlosseri also very common, either growing as a crust or as free lobes. Bryozoan turf - especially Bugula spp. Hydroids included Halecium spp. Clavelina lepadiformis also common. Very few sponges (except Leucosolenia) or anemones (except Urticina in sheltered pockets ). Loose, mobile pebbles, were widespread, remaining mostly uncolonised except for Pmatoceros spp. and filamentous algae - small growths. Few fish - no wrasse or cod family. Plaice were most conspicuous and a few others associated in holes. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Aplidium proliferum: Irregular bedrock with rounded tops, shallow gullies and fissures and crevices. No obvious orientation of features. Kelp forest dominated. (Laminaria hyperborea) with stipes about 1.5 m long. The depth was about 3.5 m bcd. Understorey of foliose red algae on all upper surfaces (not exceeding 300 mm ) the dominant animal was Polyclinum aurantium with much smaller numbers of other tunicates, small sponges and other hydroids. There were few bryozoans in amongst this turf. Kelp stipes were extensively colonised by Halichondria panicea and a number of spider crabs Hyas araneus observed clutching the stipes. Most fronds were uncolonised though some were covered with Membranipora membranacea. There was a distinct drop of 1 m to habitat 2. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Aplidium proliferum: Irregular bedrock with rounded tops, shallow gullies and fissures and crevices. No obvious orientation of features. Kelp forest dominated. (Laminaria hyperborea) with stipes about 1.5 m long. The depth was about 3.5 m bcd. Understorey of foliose red algae on all upper surfaces (not exceeding 300 mm ) the dominant animal was Polyclinum aurantium with much smaller numbers of other tunicates, small sponges and other hydroids. There were few bryozoans in amongst this turf. Kelp stipes were extensively colonised by Halichondria panicea and a number of spider crabs Hyas araneus observed clutching the stipes. Most fronds were uncolonised though some were covered with Membranipora membranacea. There was a distinct drop of 1 m to habitat 2. Ahnfeltia plicata: Generally flat bedrock plain, with little vertical growth. Wide bedrock terraces apparently flat but infact gentlyy shelving. Vertical faces up to 500 cm high facing east-north east, the width of the terraces about 5 m. Upper surfaces fairly bare with many small holes but no sign of live animals, mostly sediment filled. All colonies in upper surfaces small i.e. Flustra foliacea, Tubularia spp. and Alcyonium digitatum. The vertical surfaces swere extensively bored but again no live animals. There were many brittle stars in the holes and a rich faunal turf including many large hydroids, Diphasia pinnata and smaller species. Halecium spp. and Bugula spp., and a few Alcyonium digitatum and Sagartia spp. Small crabs in amonst the turf and larger crabs were seen in the crevices. No algae at all. There were small rounded ridges in the rock platform with no apparently uniformity of direction and no particular variety of species although wide gully bottom betweeen ridges some times bare and current swept. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Only sparse algal cover at this site - Plocamium cartilagineum, Delessaria sanguinea, Taonia atomaria and others. Laminaria hyperborea - one single plant, small and on top of a boulder. One of the most prominant animals as Flustra foliacea, often almost completely covering the sides of boulders. The other was Polyclinum aurantium, occuring in dense mats in places. There was in many places a well developed faunal turf especially common were Bugula spp., Setularia spp., Clavelina lepadiformis and also hydroids including Halecium spp., Archidistoma aggregatum also common in places - mat like straggly Alcyonidium spp. especially on the boulder tops. The flat bedrock was noticeably less well colonised - considerably scouring must occur, but spionid worms in evidence; small silty tubes strewn on the rock surface. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Large and small cobbles with an occasional boulder covered in a thin layer of silt that became thick in places. The occasional large and small boulders had kelp on the tops. Flustra foliacea on the sides orientated to the current. Scrupocellaria spp.,B otrylloides leachi and Bugula spp. were also present. Red algae were abundant all over the site. Species included Calliblepharis ciliata, Taonia atomaria, Ceramium rubrum and Phyllophora pseudoceranoides. Faunal species included Flustra foliacea, Henricia spp., Asterias rubens, Carcinus maenas and Cancer pagurus. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Chalk wave cut platform with numerous boulders of varying sizes. Small boulders and some bedrock with branching red algae, larger boulders densely covered with Flustra. Whole area was current swept. Algal cover 25 % in dense patches - algae mainly Delesseria sanguinea, Ceramium spp. and others. The bedrock areas have dense patches of Polydora spp. Bare rock was bored by Cliona spp. and Zirfaea crispata (empty holes plentiful and have extracted Venerupis saxatilis ). Small stones have Pomatoceros spp. and Ophiothrix fargalis undernaeth - near vertical surfaces have Ophiopholis aculetea arms protruding. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Uppermost parts with a few kelp plants and some red algae, especialy Phyllophora spp., Schottera nicaeensis and Rhodymenia holmsii at the bases of the outcrops. The vertical surfaces were dominated by a faunal turf. Crisia spp. were abundant and covered substantial areas. Polyclinum spp. was also abundant. Aplidium punctum was noticeably more common than on horizontal surfaces . Aplidium proliferum was also common. Myxilla incrustans was also conspicuous in places. Bugula spp. present (especially Bugula turbinata). Few hydroid varieties but Alcyonium digitatum rare at this depth/site. Numerou Ophiothrix fragilis hidden within the tops of the crevices. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Aplidium proliferum: Rock outcrops up to 6 m high. Sides are vertical, steep sloping or overhanging. Typically lowest half metre rounded and scoured clean by wave and cobble action. Rock strata horizontally bedded providing lots of deep clefts up to 2 m in and 30 cm high. Often runnuing continuously along considerable legnths of rock. Tops of rock, kelp forest with foliose red algal understorey. The upper vertical faces of rock were dominated by the foliose algae. Upper overhanging face had a rich faunal turf - unusually for the area based on sponges esp. Halichondria panicea. Rocks riddled with holes, many occupied by Hiatella arctica or Ophiothrix fragilis. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Small crabs amongst the turf -generally disguised species such as Macropodia rostrata, Inachus phalangium and Hyas coarctatus. Ahnfeltia plicata: Thin kelp forest at 0.5 - 2.5 m with some red algae, which was not very profuse. Holdfasts generally small with Crisia spp. and Botryllus schlosseri being the main inhabitant. Other fauna on horizontal surfaces tended to be sparse and erratic, these species included Tubularia spp., Alcyonium digitatum and Botryllus schlosseri. The vertical components of the bedrock had a rich and varied animal turf with species incuding Bugula spp. and Clavelina lepadiformis. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

The most common foliose algae was Plocamium cartilagineum. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Kelp forest at 1.3 m acd. Laminaria hyperborea commo nwith a carpet of red algae underneath. Reds mainly Polysiphonia, species again mainly covered with Modiolus modiolus, Furcellaria lumbricalis found in areas where there was much sand accumulated. In general the rock was covered with a thin film of coarse sand. The kelp stipes, were covered with Palmaria palmata (common) along with Polysiphonia sp., Ceramium rubrum, Membranoptera alata and encrusting algae. The vertical surfaces supported algae towards the top but faunal turf dominated 75% of the cover. Amongst this turf a few filamentous weeds were found. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Larger, more stable boulders and cobbles. These were more densely colonised by growths of foliose and filamentous red and brown algjae including Laminaria saccharina. On the sides of these cobbles there were turf species including Clavelina lepadiformis, Leucosolenia spp., Flustra foliacea and Bugula spp. Underneath were prawns and Ophiothrix fraglis. Also obsereved were a few large detached Botryllus schlosseri. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: The boulder tops had an algal turf, mainly Ceramium rubrum, Delesseria sanguinea, Plocamium cartilagineum and much Electra pilosa. Large patches of Alcyonidium diaphanum and Laminaria hyperborea onl arge boulders. The sides of the boulders were covered in sea- squirts such as Botryllus schlosseri, Morchelium argus, Aplidium proliferatum common. Also Crisia eburnea, Bugula flabellata and Halecium spp.. Small boulders have Laminaria saccharina (presumably less stable boulders). NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.
