Ahnfeltia plicata: Low flat platforms of chalk with dense Polydora spp. turf and large patches of Taonia atomaria, with occasional Delesseria, Hypoglossum hypoglossoides, Plocamium cartilagineum and Audouinella spp. Also sand with Sabellaria spinulosa. There was a kelp forest with a dense understorey of foliose algae. Polydora spp. in silt; Urticina felina in the crevices formed by sculpturing. Vertical faces were dominated by rbyozoans (plus some sponges and Aplidium punctum), the ledges again were dominated by Polydora spp. Further offshore flat reefs, with the fauna gradually becoming similar to that of the offshore dive sites. Few reds, but Flustra foliacea appears and bryozoan turf gets thicker. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Aplidium proliferum: Wave cut platform in chalk with low ledges. Main feature of this habitat was the rich faunal turf on cobbles and boulders. Good diversity and % cover of bryozoans (esp. Flustra foliacea, Bugula spp. and Bicellaria spp.), hydroids (Halecium ciliata and Campanularia verticillatus) and tunicates (including Clavelina lepadiformis, Polyclinum aurautium, Botryllus sclosseri, Botrylloides leachi, with lesser quantities of Aplidium spp. and Sidnyum spp.). A few sponges including Leucosolenia spp. on rocks, Cliona spp. boring into rocks and Polydora spp. evident in bare patches of bedrock. Alcyonidium diaphanum was abundant on most substrates especilly on bare rock. Mobile fauna was mainly Asterias rubens and crabs (Cancer pagurus was very common) and suprisingly Carcinus maenas (2 specimens were seen). NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Kelp forest 2.9 m bcd, Laminaria hyperborea common on the reef tops being easy to swim through. Various Polysiphonia sp. were found to be common on the rocks along with Plocamium cartilagineum. The Polysiphonia spp. were found to be encrusted with Modiolus spp. Coarse sand was found as a thin covering of the rocks. The kelp stipes were covered with a variety of reds including Membranoptera alata. Phycodrys rubens, Plocamium cartilagineum and Ptilota plumosa. The rock faces had reds on the upper parts but the middle and bottoms were dominated by the animal fauna typical of the area, sponges, bryozoans and tunicates. NOTE: Substratum %f data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Broken rock platform consisting of lumps( of rock) >1 m wide, flat topped, with gullies and channels between. The channels were up to 0.5 m deep. In the base of channel, on the bedrock were pebbles, fine gravel and silt. A layer of flocculent silt was trapped over the whole surface of the rock. No seaweeds were seen and the rock was dominated by patches of Flustra foliacea and other bryozoans. Alcyonium digitatum and Asterias rubens were abundant. A few other bryozoans and hydroids were found but surveying was difficult due to extremely poor visibility. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

In the base of channel, on the bedrock were pebbles, fine gravel and silt. A layer of flocculent silt was trapped over the whole surface of the rock. No seaweeds were seen and the rock was dominated by patches of Flustra foliacea and other bryozoans. Alcyonium digitatum and Asterias rubens were abundant. A few other bryozoans and hydroids were found but surveying was difficult due to extremely poor visibility. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Low flat platforms of chalk with dense Polydora spp. turf and large patches of Taonia atomaria, with occasional Delesseria, Hypoglossum hypoglossoides, Plocamium cartilagineum and Audouinella spp. Also sand with Sabellaria spinulosa. There was a kelp forest with a dense understorey of foliose algae. Polydora spp. in silt; Urticina felina in the crevices formed by sculpturing. Vertical faces were dominated by bryozoans (plus some sponges and Aplidium punctum), the ledges again were dominated by Polydora spp. Further offshore flat reefs, with the fauna gradually becoming similar to that of the offshore dive sites. Few reds, but Flustra foliacea appears and bryozona turf gets thicker. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Laminaria hyperborea on part of a reef/terrace system. Tatty, small plants with small fronds. The rocks covered with Delesseria sanguinea and Plocamium cartilagineum and this algae was densely overgrown with Electra pilosa. Kelp stipes again overgrown with a similar biota. The habitat seemed subject to strong wave action as there were rock mills presetn. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Horizontal surfaces of large boulders and bedrock. Irregular bedrock with shallow gullies in kelp forest. Understorey of red weeds e.g. Delessaria sanguinea and large patches of Polyclinum aurantium. This soon gave way to more broken ground with deeper gullies and vertical edges of bedrock with fewer red weeds and a very rich animal turf. All species recorded for the area were present with no obviuos habitat preference. Occasional flat topped plateux, about 1 - 2 m above the general bottom and had sparse kelp cover with a more varied understorey of red weeds and faunal turf. Undercut edges had numerous crabs and a few lobsters and also one leopard spotted goby. NOTE: Substratum % data are onverted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: The dominant encrusting species was Halichondria panicea in extensive sheets. Electra pilosa was found towards the base of the stipes. Small Cancer pagurus, Asterias rubens and Henricia spp. climbing someway up. Dynamema pumila common at the base of the stipes. The common algae inculded Phycodrys rubens, Membranoptera alata, Plocamium cartilagineum, and Ceramium spp.(Sub-habitat of 145.042.001) NOTE: Substratum % data are converted fromi the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Flat topped platform dissected by surge gullies generally about 600 mm deep and 1.5 m wide. Flat areas dominated by Flustra foliacea, bryozoan turf, Polyclinum aurantium and Cliona spp. The shallow sided gullies had Alcyonium digitatum commono n the top edges, otherwise similar cover to the flat areas. Rounded gully bottom - with no life and clean of sediment. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.
Aplidium proliferum: Flat topped platform dissected by surge gullies generally about 600 mm deep and 1.5 m wide. Flat areas dominated by Flustra foliacea, bryozoan turf, Polyclinum aurantium and Cliona spp. The shallow sided gullies had Alcyonium digitatum common on the top edges, otherwise similar cover to the flat areas. Ahnfeltia plicata: The habitat is divided into niches, bedrock at reef face covered with extensive Polydora spp. The reef face and edges consist of dense stands of Alcyonium digitatum on Flustra foliacea, with bryozoan and hydroid turf interspersed. Small stones bored by Cliona spp. and the large boulders dominated by Flustra foliacea. The turf is maipnly Crisia eburnea and Scrupocellaria scruposa. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Small stones bored by Cliona spp. and the large boulders dominated by Flustra foliacea. The turf is mainly Crisia eburnea and Scrupocellaria scruposa. Ahnfeltia plicata: Small rocks were covered with algae, mixed with some Halecium spp. Flatter rocks were covered with Polydora spp. and silt. Clavelina lepadiformis forms many small clumps on many small rocks. There were pebbles with Pomatoceros spp. Sand and gravel was colonised with Sabella spp. and Lanice conchilega (but not very many). Boulders have more bryozoa (Flustra foliacea and small tufts of Bicellaria ciliata, Crisia spp. and Bugula spp.) than red algae. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.
Aplidium proliferum: Bedrock surfaces formed from various gullies, bedrock and small boulders. Occasional large boulders with sharply undercut edges in depressions in the bedrock. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Rock platform, below reef face with polychaete tubes, mainly Sabellaria spinulosa and silt. Reef face a mixture of bryozoan /hydroid turf either Alcyonium digitatum or Flustra foliacea in dense satnds. Bryozoan turf slightly different with Eucratea loricata and Vesicularia spinosa joining Scrupocellaria spp. and Bugula spp. Small stones covered with Cliona spp. which appears to prefer this to the bedrock. The stones were also covered with Pomatoceros and the encrusting bryozoan Escharella immersa. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Flat bedrock plain, at inner edge featureless with no crevices/ cracks etc. towards the boundary of habitat 3 becoming fissured with irregular cracks generally about 100 mm deep. There was a thin covering of coarse sand in parts and foliose algae ( Polysiphonia nigra and Plocamium cartilagineum). Attached animal cover was very limited, Polyclinum aurantium again was again very common as was Haliclona oculata and Alcyonidium diaphanum and Urticina felina was in crevices. There was a slight rise to habitat 3. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original subcstratum 1-3 scoring system.

Aplidium proliferum: Vertical surfaces - animal dominated (see habitat 5) and with stands of plants on illuminated surfaces. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: The whole site appeared to be one large single habitat, despite the small pckets of sediment as theses did not appear to be colonised. Unstable pebbles also remained uncolonised, but most of the rocks were large enough to support a fairly dense turf of faunal species, especially the moderate sized boulders. Several species were conspicuous and common includig Clavelina lepadiformis in lumps and masses, Alcyonidium spp. (single pieces, widespread especially on tops of rocks), Flustra foliacea on (tops/sides) of the rocks. Amongst the hydroids Halecium spp. was obvious, and the larger stouter Abietenaria spp. Bryozoans were abundant often dominating the boulder surfaces. Apart from Flustra foliacea the soecies involved were Bugula flabellata and Bugula plumosa. The small blobby tunicate Archidistoma aggregatum abundant. Other species seen sporadically included the boring phase of Cliona spp., especially on low, small boulders, one or two clumps of Alcyonium digitatum. Rarely Sabella spp., protruding from sides of small boulders or silty sand pockets. rticina felina was occasionally seen, on the bedrock boulder floor. Amongst mobile fauna - the commonest were young Asterias rubens, Hyas spp. and several Cancer pagurus and Pleuronectes spp. Otherwise few fish, just Callionymus lyra. Crossaster papposus seen, and sevral Janolus cristatus were seen feeding on Bugula spp. Algae virtually non existant - just a few unidentified reds.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Kelp forest on flat bedrock platform. Rock surfaec dominated by mats of young Modiolus modiolus mussels, with Asterias rubens feeding on them. Also Urticina felina and Helcion pellucidum and Membranipora membranacea on the kelp fronds. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.
Aplidium proliferum: Kelp forest on flat bedrock platform. Rock surface dominated by mats of young Modiolus modiolus mussels, with Asterias rubens feeding on them. Also Urticina felina and Helcion pellucidum and Membranipora membranacea on the kelp fronds. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Bedrock with widely separated terraces and much rock debris. The horizontal surfaces were densely fringed with Flustra foliacea (30 - 60 cm from the edges of outcrops and terraces). The other horizontal areas had a more mixed turf of Crisia spp., Leucosolenai spp. and Polyclinum aurantium. Vertical and more steeply sloping faces similar but generally more Bugula spp., Clavelina lepadiformis and Diphasia spp. Areas of freshly broken rock often but not always, exposed piddocks and had numerous crabs Cancer pagurus and a few squat lobsters Galathea strigosa. Faunal turf generally less dense than in other sites dived in this region, with areas of uncolonised, or thinly colonised rock, usually with boring Cliona spp. visible. Some smaller unstable stones covered with Pomatoceros spp. and prawns underneath. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Generally flat bedrock plain, with little vertical growth. Wide bedrock terraces apparently flat but infact gently shelving. Vertical faces up to 500 cm high facing east-north east, the width of the terraces about 5 m. Upper surfsaces fairly bare with many small holes but no sign of live animals, mostly sediment filled. All colonies in upper surfaces small i.e. Flustra foliacea, Tubularia spp. and Alcyonium digitatum. The vertical surfaces swere extensively bored but again no live animals. There were many brittle stars in the holes and a rich faunal turf including many large hydroids, Diphasia pinnata and smaller species. Halecium spp. and Bugula spp., and a few Alcyonium digitatum and Sagartia spp. Small crabs in amonst the turf and larger crabs were seen in the crevices. No algae at all. There were small rounded ridges in the rock platform with no apparently uniformity of direction and no particular variety of species although wide gully bottom betweeen ridges some times bare and current swept. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Large and small cobbles with an occasional boulder covered in a thin layer of silt that became thick in places. The occasional large and small boulders had kelp on the tops. Flustra foliacea on the sides orientated to the current. Scrupocellaria spp., Botrylloides leachi and Bugula spp. were also present. Red algae were abundant all over the site. Species included Calliblepahris ciliata, Taonia atomaria, Ceramium rubrum and Phyllophora pseudoceranoides. Faunal species included Flustra foliacea, Henricia spp., Asterias rubens, Carcinus maenas and Cancer pagurus. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Aplidium proliferum: The bedrock was densely covered with kelp forest. Large plants with stipes 2 - 3 m tall, and smaller ones in between. Often the holdfasts were holding each other. The dominant component of the animal turf was Polyclinum aurantium. Aplidium punctum was also common, and other species such as Botryllus schlosseri and Alcyonidium diaphanum. Urticina felina was found in depressions. Asterias rubens and Henricia spp. were common. Algae were encrusted with Electra pilosa NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Starting about 1 m from base were Laminaria hyperborea plants. Interspaced between and beneath was a turf of bryozoans and a variety of tunicates. Perophora listeri more abundant here than at other sites. Sidnyum spp. also much in evidence, wityh clumps of Aplidium punctum. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Perophora listeri more abundant here than at other sites. Sidnyum spp. also much in evidence, with clumps of Aplidium punctum. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: The main difference at this site was the abundance of Alcyonium digitatum - greater than in other areas; possibly due to the greater stability of rocks here. Some Polyclinum aurantium and condsiderable patches of Archidistoma aggregatum - as at other sites. Flustra foliacea also abundant on current swept surfaces. No sponges recorded except for Cliona spp. Turf difficult to distinuish much, but Halecium spp. and Bugula plumosa prominent, also Clavelina lepadiformis. Vertical surfaces silty - less colonisation. On low lying bedrock there were often areas densely packed with a >cm thick mat of tubicolous amphipods. Sometimes uninterrupted; or with algae/small hydroids showing through. Very silty. Urticina spp. protruding in places. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Aplidium proliferum: Horizontal surfaces of large boulders and bedrock. Irregular bedrock with shallow gullies in kelp forest. Understorey of red weeds e.g. Delessaria sanguinea and large patches of Polyclinum aurantium. This soon gave way to more broken ground with deeper gullies and vertical edges of bedrock with fewer red weeds and a very rich animal turf. All species recorded for the area were present with no obviuos habitat preference. Occasional flat topped plateux, about 1 - 2 m above the general bottom and had sparse kelp cover with a more varied understorey of red weeds and faunal turf. Undercut edges had numerous crabs and a few lobsters and also one leopard spotted goby. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Aplidium proliferum: Wave cut platform in chalk with low ledges. Main feature of this habitat was the rich faunal turf on cobbles and boulders. Good diversity and % cover of bryozoans (esp. Flustra foliacea, Bugula spp. and Bicellaria spp.), hydroids (Halecium ciliata and Campanularia verticillatus) and tunicates (including Clavelina lepadiformis, Polyclinum aurautium, Botryllus sclosseri, Botrylloides leachi, with lesser quantities of Aplidium spp. and Sidnyum spp.). A few sponges including Leucosolenia spp. on rocks, Cliona spp. boring into rocks and Polydora spp. evident in bare patches of bedrock. Alcyonidium diaphanum was abundant on most substrates especilly on bare rock. Mobile fauna was mainly Asterias rubens and crabs (Cancer pagurus was very common) and suprisingly Carcinus maenas (2 specimens were seen). NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Aplidium proliferum: Generally flat bedrock plain, with little vertical growth. Wide bedrock terraces apparently flat but infact gently shelving. Vertical faces up to 500 cm high facing east-north east, the width of the terraces about 5 m. Upper surfaces fairly bare with many small holes but no sign of live animals, mostly sediment filled. All colonies in upper surfaces small i.e. Flustra foliacea, Tubularia spp. and Alcyonium digitatum. The vertical surfaces swere extensively bored but again no live animals. There were many brittle stars in the holes and a rich faunal turf including many large hydroids, Diphasia pinnata and smaller species. Halecium spp. and Bugula spp., and a few Alcyonium digitatum and Sagartia spp. Small crabs in amonst the turf and larger crabs were seen in the crevices. No algae at all. There were small rounded ridges in the rock platform with no apparently uniformity of direction and no particular variety of species although wide gully bottom betweeen ridges some times bare and current swept. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Aplidium proliferum: Only sparse algal cover at this site - Plocamium cartilagineum, Delessaria sanguinea, Taonia atomaria and others. Laminaria hyperborea - one single plant, small and on top of a boulder. One of the most prominant animals was Flustra foliacea, often almost completely covering the sides of boulders. The other was Polyclinum aurantium, occuring in dense mats in places. There was in many places a well developed faunal turf especially common were Bugula spp., Setularia spp., Clavelina lepadiformis and also hydroids including Halecium spp., Archidistoma aggregatum also common in places - mat like straggly Alcyonidium spp. especially on the boulder tops. The flat bedrock was noticeably less well colonised - considerably scouring must occur, but spionid worms in evidence; small silty tubes strewn on the rock surface. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Kelp forest 2.9 m bcd, Laminaria hyperborea common on the reef tops being easy to swim through. Various Polysiphonia sp. were found to be common on the rocks along with Plocamium cartilagineum. The Polysiphonia spp. were found to be encrusted with Modiolus spp. Coarse sand was found as a thin covering of the rocks. The kelp stipes were covered with a variety of reds including Membranoptera alata. Phycodrys rubens, Plocamium cartilagineum and Ptilota plumosa. The rock faces had reds on the upper parts but the middle and bottoms were dominated by the animal fauna typical of the area, sponges, bryozoans and tunicates. Aplidium proliferum: Rough ground of chalk bedrock plain with boulders, pebbles and cobbles. Current swept and the greatest cover was by red foliose and filamentous algae (e.g. Plocamium cartilagineum). These grew on the tops of smaller cobbles and boulders. Some of the larger boulders in particular were animal dominated. A faunal turf varying from clumps of Flustra foliacea, to growths of Alcyonidium spp. in various forms ranging from long, thin to considerably branched. Botryllus schlosseri also very common, either growing as a crust or as free lobes. Bryozoan turf - especially Bugula spp. Hydroids included Halecium spp. Clavelina lepadiformis also common. Very few sponges (except Leucosolenia) or anemones (except Urticina in sheltered pockets ). Loose, mobile pebbles, were widespread, remaining mostly uncolonised except for Pomatoceros spp. and filamentous algae - small growths. Few fish - no wrasse or cod family. Plaice were most conspicuous and a few others associated in holes. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Flat bedrock plain, at inner edge featureless with no crevices/ cracks etc. towards the boundary of habitat 3 becoming fissured with irregular cracks generally about 100 mm deep. Attached animal cover was very limited, Polyclinum aurantium again was again very common as was Haliclona oculata and Alcyonidium diaphanum and Urticinas felina was in crevices. Ahnfeltia plicata: Low flat platforms of chalk with dense Polydora spp. turf and large patches of Taonia atomaria, with occasional Delesseria, Hypoglossum hypoglossoides, Plocamium cartilagineum and Audouinella spp. Also sand with Sabellaria spinulosa. There was a kelp forest with a dense understorey of foliose algae. Polydora spp. in silt; Urticina felina in the crevices formed by sculpturing. Vertical faces were dominated by bryozoans (plus some sponges atnd Aplidium punctum), the ledges again were dominated by Polydora spp. Further offshore flat reefs, with the fauna gradually becoming similar to that of the offshore dive sites. Few reds, but Flustra foliacea appears and bryozoan turf gets thicker. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Bedrock with widely separated terraces and much rock debris. The horizontal surfaces were densely fringed with Flustra foliacea (30 - 60 cm from the edges of outcrops and terraces). The other horizontal areas had a more mixed turf of Crisia spp., Leucosolenia spp. and Polyclinum aurantium. Vertical and more steeply sloping faces similar but generally more Bugula spp., Clavelina lepadiformis and Diphasia spp. Areas of freshly broken rock often but not always, exposed piddocks and had numerous crabs Cancer pagurus and a few squat lobsters Galathea strigosa. Faunal turf generally less dense than in other sites dived in this region, with areas of uncolonised, or thinly colonised rock, usually with boring Cliona spp. visible. Some smaller unstablestones covered with Pomatoceros spp. and prawns underneath. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Laminaria hyperborea on part of a reef/terrace system. Tatty, small plants with small fronds. The rocks covered with Delesseria sanguinea and Plocamium cartilagineum and this algae was densely overgrown with Electra pilosa. Kelp stipes again overgrown with a similar biota. The habitat seemed subject to strong wave action as therewere rock mills present. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Small rocks were covered with algae, mixed with some Halecium spp. Flatter rocks were covered with Polydora spp. and silt. Clavelina lepadiformis forms many small clumps on many small rocks. There were pebbles with Pomatoceros spp. Sand and gravel was colonised with Sabella spp. and Lanice conchilega (but not very many). Boulders have more bryozoa (Flustra foliacea and small tufts of Bicellaria ciliata, Crisia spp. and Bugula spp.) than red algae. Aplidium proliferum: Kelp forest on flat bedrock platform. Rock surface dominated by mats of young Modiolus modiolus mussels, with Asterias rubens feeding on them. Also Urticina felina and Helcion pellucidum and Membranipora membranacea on the kelp fronds. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Aplidium proliferum: Large and small cobbles with an occasional boulder covered in a thin layer of silt that became thick in places. The occasional large and small boulders had kelp on the tops. Flustra foliacea on the sides orientated to the current. Scrupocellaria spp., Botrylloides leachi and Bugula spp. were also present. Red algae were abundant all over the site. Species included Calliblepharis ciliata, Taonia atomaria, Ceramium rubrum and Phyllophora pseudoceranoides. Faunal species included Flustra foliacea, Henricia spp., Asterias rubens, Carcinus maenas and Cancer pagurus. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.
Breil Nook (200 - 300 m off headland) (Flamborough Headland)

Ahnfeltia plicata: Broken rock platform consisting of lumps (of rock) >1 m wide, flat topped, with gullies and channels between. The channels were up to 0.5 m deep. In the base of channel, on the bedrock were pebbles, finte gravel and silt. A layer of flocculent silt was trapped over the whole surface of the rock. No seaweeds were seen and the rock was dominated by patches of Flustra foliacea and other bryozoans. Alcyonium digitatum and Asterias rubens were abundant. A few other bryozoans and hydroids were found but surveying was difficult due to extremely poor visibility. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Aplidium proliferum: Generally flat bedrock plain, with little vertical growth. Wide bedrock terraces apparently flat but infact gently shelving. Vertical faces up to 500 cm high facing east-north east, the width of the terraces about 5 m. Upper surfaces fairly bare with many small holes but no sign of live animals, mostly sediment filled. All colonies in upper surfaces small i.e. Flustra foliacea, Tubularia spp. and Alcyonium digitatum. The vertical surfaces swere extensively bored but again no live animals. There were many brittle stars in the holes and a rich faunal turf including many large hydroids, Diphasia pinnata and smaller species. Halecium spp. and Bugula spp., and a few Alcyonium digitatum and Sagartia spp. Small crabs in amonst the turf and larger crabs were seen in the crevices. No algae at all. There were small rounded ridges in the rock platform with no apparently uniformity of direction and no particular variety of species although wide gully bottom betweeen ridges some times bare and current swept. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: The habitat was uniform throughout the dive (approx. 250 m in length). The chalk humps raised approximately 250 - 500 mm above the hollows. 100% covered with mainly Polyclinum aurantium, wihth tufts of Flustra foliacea common: also seasquirt Archidistoma aggregatum and hydroids and bryozoans in patches especially around the side of the humps. The hollows were a mixture of chalk bare bedrock with piddocks (approx. 10/0.5 x 0.5 m): also a covering of silt tubes of Polydora spp. (and possibly Fabricia sabella and Cliona celata boring. These hollows had some small stones with Pomatoceros spp. and Ophiothrix fragalis. The minature reefs had overhangs with edible crabs, lobster and Galathea strigosa. Any vertical surface (only 10 cm ! usually) with brittle star limbs out of holes (probably Ophiactis balli ). No algae were seen. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: The boulder tops had an algal turf, mainly Ceramium rubrum, Delesseria sanguinea, Plocamium cartilagineum and much Electra pilosa. Large patches of Alcyonidium diaphanum and Laminaria hyperborea on large boulders. The sides of the boulders were covered in sea- squirts such as Botryllus schlosseri, Morchelium argus, Aplidium proliferatum common. Also Crisia eburnea, Bugula flabellata and Halecium spp.. Small boulders have Laminaria saccharna (presumably less stable boulders). NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Wave cut platform imn chalk with low ledges. Main feature of this habitat was the rich faunal turf on cobbles and boulders. Good diversity and % cover of bryozoans (esp. Flustra foliacea, Bugula spp. and Bicellaria spp.), hydroids (Halecium ciliata and Campanularia verticillatus) and tunicates (including Clavelina lepadiformis, Polyclinum aurautium, Botryllus sclosseri, Botrylloides leachi, with lesser quantities of Aplidium spp. and Sidnyum spp.). A few sponges including Leucosolenia spp. on rocks, Cliona spp. boring into rocks and Polydora spp. evident in bare patches of bedrock. Alcyonidium diaphanum was abundant on most substrates especilly on bare rock. Mobile fauna was mainly Asterias rubens and crabs (Cancer pagurus was very common) and suprisingly Carcinus maenas (2 specimens were seen). NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Tops of outcrops had tatty kelp, the plants smaller than 1 m were overgrown with Obelia spp. and Membranipora mambranacea. The stipes were encrusted with Electra pilosa and Halichondria paniceaa nd red algae. Areas between the kelp with a range of turf species and encrusting species. There were large patches of Myxilla incrustans and Halichondria panicea. Other species included Clavelina lepadiformis, Aplidium spp. and Botrylus schlosseri common smaller species included Crisia spp. and Hiatella arctica. Noticeably fewer Alcyonium digitatum and Flustra foliacea than in deeper sites and more sponges. The sides of the outcrops had fewer algae than elsewhere. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Aplidium proliferum: Kelp forest 2.9 m bcd, Laminaria hyperborea common on the reef tops being easy to swim through. Various Polysiphonia sp. were found to be common on the rocks along with Plocamium cartilagineum. The Polysiphonia spp. were found to be encrusted with Modiolus spp. Coarse sand was found as a thin covering of the rocks. The kelp stipes were covered with a variety of reds including Membranoptera alata. Phycodrys rubens, Plocamium cartilagineum and Ptilota plumosa. The rock faces had reds on the upper parts but the middle and bottoms were dominated by the animal fauna typical of the area, sponges, bryozoans and tunicates. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Flat bedrock plain, at inner edge featureless with no crevices/ cracks etc. towards the boundary of habitat 3 becoming fissured with irregular cracks generally about 100 mm deep. There was a thin covering of coarse sand in parts and foliose algae ( Polysiphonia nigra and Plocamium cartilagineum). Attached animal cover was very limited, Polyclinum aurantium again was again very common as wasHaliclona oculata and Alcyonidium diaphanum and Urticina felina was in crevices. There was a slight rise to habitat 3. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Rock outcrops up to 6 m high. Sides are vertical, steep sloping or overhanging. Typically lowest half metre rounded and scoured clean by wave and cobble action. Rock strata horizontally rbedded providing lots of deep clefts up to 2 m in and 30 cm high. Often runnuing continuously along considerable legnths of rock. Tops of rock, kelp forest with foliose red algal understorey. The upper vertical faces of rock were dominated by the foliose algae. Upper overhanging face had a rich faunal turf - unusually for the area based on sponges esp. Halichondria panicea. Rocks riddled with holes, many occupied by Hiatella arctica or Ophiothrix fragilis. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Rough ground of chalk bedrock plain with boulders, pebbles and cobbles. Current swept and the greatest cover was by red foliose and filamentous algae (e.g. Plocamium cartilagineum). These grew on the tops of smaller cobbles and boulders. Some of the larger boulders in particular were animal dominated. A faunal turf varying from clumps of Flustra foliacea, to growths of Alcyonidium spp. in various forms ranging from long, thisn to considerably branched. Botryllus schlosseri also very common, either growing as a crust or as free lobes. Bryozoan turf - especially Bugula spp. Hydroids included Halecium spp. Clavelina lepadiformis also common. Very few sponges (except Leucosolenia) or anemones (except Urticina in sheltered pockets ). Loose, mobile pebbles, were widespread, remaining mostly uncolonised except for Pomatoceros spp. and filamentous algae - small growths. Few fish - no wrasse or cod family. Plaice were most conspicuous and a few others associated in holes. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Aplidium proliferum: Only sparse algal cover at this site - Plocamium cartilagineum, Delessaria sanguinea, Taonia atomaria and others. Laminaria hyperborea - one single plant, small and on top of a boulder. One of the most prominant animals was Flustra foliacea, often almost completely covering the sides of boulders. The other was Polyclinum aurantium, occuring in dense mats in places. There was in many places a well developed faunal turf especially common were Bugula spp., Setularia spp., Clavelina lepadiformis and also hydroids including Halecium spp., Archidistoma aggregatum also common in places - mat like straggly Alcyonidium spp. especially on the boulder tops. The flat bedrock was noticeably less well colonised - considerably scouring must occur, but spionid worms in evidence; small silty tubes strewn on the rock surface. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Broken rock platform consisting of lumps (of rock) >1 m wide, flat topped, with gullies and channels between. The channels were up to 0.5 m deep. In the base of channel, on the bedrock were pebbles, fine gravel and silt. A layer of flocculent silt was trapped over the whole surface of the rock. No seaweeds were seen and the rock was dominated by patches of Flustra foliacea and other bryozoans. Alcyonium digitatum and Asterias rubens were abundant. A few other bryozoans and hydroids were found but surveying waas difficult due to extremely poor visibility. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Aplidium proliferum: Wave cut platform in chalk with low ledges. Main feature of this habitat was the rich faunal turf on cobbles and boulders. Good diversity and % cover of bryozoans (esp. Flustra foliacea, Bugula spp. and Bicellaria spp.), hydroids (Halecium ciliata and Campanularia verticillatus) and tunicates (including Clavelina lepadiformis, Polyclinum aurautium, Botryllus sclosseri, Botrylloides leachi, with lesser quantities of Aplidium spp. and Sidnyum spp.). A few sponges including Leucosolenia spp. on rocks, Cliona spp. boring into rocks and Polydora spp. evident in bare patches of bedrock. Alcyonidium diaphanum was abundant on most substrates especilly on bare rock. Mobile fauna was mainly Asterias rubens and crabs (Cancer pagurus was very common) and suprisingly Carcinus maenas (2 specimens were seen). Aplidium proliferum: The whole site appeared to be one large single habitat, despite the small pockets of sediment as these did not appear to be colonised. Unstable pebbles also remained uncolonised, but most of the rocks were large enough to support a fairly dense turf of faunal species, especially the moderate sized boulders. Several species were conspicuous and common including Clavelina lepadiformis in lumps and masses, Alcyonidium spp. (single pieces, widespread especially on tops of rocks), Flustra foliacea (tops/sides) of the rocks. Amongst the hydroids Halecium spp. was obvious, and the larger stouter Abietenaria spp. Bryozoans were abundant often dominating the boulder surfaces. Apart from Flustra foliacea the species involved were Bugula flabellata and Bugula plumosa. The small blobby tunicate Archidistoma abundant. Other species seen sporadically included the boring phase of Cliona spp., especially on low, small boulders, one or 2 clumps of Alcyonium digitatum. Rarely Sabella spp., protruding from sides of small boulders or silty sand pockets. Urticina felina was occasionally seen, on the bedrock boulder floor. Amongst mobile fauna - the commonest were young Asterias rubens, Hyas spp. and several Cancer pagurus and Pleuronectes spp. Otherwise few fish, just Callionymus lyra. Crossaster seen, and several Janolus were seen feeding on Bugula spp. Algae virtually non existant - just a few unidentified reds. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Mixture of cobbles, boulders and pebbles with fauna typical for the area but overlain to a greater or lesser extent by coarse sand which mostly buried all but the largest cobbles and boulders - hence available hard substrate was limited and presumably affected by scour. Turf communities consisted of Flustra foliacea, Bicellaria ciliata, Halecium spp., Bugula spp., Campanularia spp. with fewer tunicates (only Clavelina lepadiformis, Botryllus schlosseri and Botrylloides leachi forming a significant part ) than elsewhere (no Polyclinum aurantium or Archidistoma aggregatum). Plenty of crabs (Carcinus maenas) and Asterias rubens in evidence with sevral large Crossaster papposus. Urticina felina common poking up through the sand. Very few weeds present mostly as small patches. A few unpleasant objects floated past or were found rotting on the seabed. NOTE: Substratum % data are convered from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Chalk wave cut platform with numerous boulders of varying sizes. Small boulders and some bedrock with branching red algae, larger boulders densely covered with Flustra. Whole area was current swept. Algal cover 25 % in dense patches - algae mainly Delesseria sanguinea, Ceramium spp. and others. The bedrock areas have dense patches of Polydora spp. Bare rock was bored by Cliona spp. and Zirfaea crispata (empty holes plentiful and have extracted Venerupis saxatilis ). Small stones have Pomatoceros spp. and Ophiothrix fragalis undernaeth - near vertical surfaces have Ophiopholis aculetea arms protruding. Aplidium proliferum: Chalk wave cut platform with numerous boulders of varying sizes. Small boulders and some bedrock with branching red algae, larger boulders densely covered with Flustra. Whole area was current swept. Algal cover 25 % in dense patches - algae mainly Delesseria sanguinea, Ceramium spp. and others. The bedrock areas have dense patches of Polydora spp. Bare rock was bored by Cliona spp. and Zirfaea crispata (empty holes plentiful and have extracted Venerupis saxatilis ). Small stones have Pomatoceros spp. and Ophiothrix fragalis undernaeth - near vertical surfaces have Ophiopholis aculetea arms protruding. Ahnfeltia plicata: Horizontal surface of chalk bedrock platform, dominated by the kelps Laminaria digitata and Laminaria hyperborea. Between kelp holfasts, almost 100% cover of red encrusting algae (Lithothamnion sp.) and a very poor animal presence. NOTE: Substratum % dataare converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Small rocks were covered with algae, mixed with some Halecium spp. Flatter rocks were covered with Polydora spp. and silt. Clavelina lepadiformis forms many small clumps on many small rocks. There were pebbles with Pomatoceros spp. Sand and gravel was colonised with Sabella spp. and Lanice conchilega (but not very many). Boulders have more bryozoa (Flustra foliacea and small tufts of Bicellaria ciliata, Crisia spp. and Bugula spp.) than red algae. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.
Aplidium proliferum: Stands of Laminaria hyperborea (common). The rock surface between were at least 50% covered by red algae. Sessile animals perhaps 20%. The rest more or less bare with fine silt. The most common foliose algae was Plocamium cartilagineum. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Bryozoan turf dominated - mostly small species especially Crisia spp. and also Bugula plumosa & Bugula flabellata were common. Hydroids included Diphasia spp. and Nemertesia spp. Good variety of mobile liefe. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.
Aplidium proliferum: Bryozoan turf dominated - mostly small species especially Crisia spp. and also Bugula plumosa & Bugula flabellata were common. Ahnfeltia plicata: Larger, more stable boulders and cobbles. These were more densely colonised by growths of foliose and filamentous red and brown algae including Laminaria saccharina. On the sides of these cobbles there were turf species including Clavelina lepadiformis, Leucosolenia spp., Flustra foliacea and Bugula spp. Underneath were prawns and Ophiothrix fraglis. Also obsereved were a gfew large detached Botryllus schlosseri. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Areas of raised bedrock approximately 300 mm above the lowe rflat surface. Uneven surface with top sides 100% coated with tunicates Polyclinum aurantium was dominant and Archidistoma aggregatum. Small numbers of sponges Haliclona oculata and Leucosolenia spp. present, edges of raised bedrock fissured and broken providing shelter for larger mobile crustaceans.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Sand scourde chalk platform. Low lying scoured bedrock - very little faunal turf decreasing down the bedrock as scour, increases. Aplidium proliferum: Bedrock plain with small channels 10 - 40 cm deep running through with a small amount of gravel/cobbles in the bottom. Rock covered in a bryozoan turf consisting of a mixture of species including Crisia spp., Bugula turbinata and Eucratea loricata. Alcyonium digitatum was abundant. Other species included the burrowing form of Cliona spp., juevenile Henricia spp., Asterias rubens, Aplidium proliferatum, Sidnyum turbinatum, Clavelina lepadiformis and Botryllus schlosseri. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Low flat platforms of chalk with dense Polydora spp. turf and large patches of Taonia atomaria, with occasional Delesseria, Hypoglossum hypoglossoides, Plocamium cartilagineum and Audouinella spp. Also sand with Sabellaria spinulosa. There was a kelp forest with a dense unrderstorey of foliose algae. Polydora spp. in silt; Urticina felina in the crevices formed by sculpturing. Vertical faces were dominated by bryozoans (plus some sponges and Aplidium punctum), the ledges again were dominated by Polydora spp. Further offshore flat reefs, with the fauna gradually becoming similar to that of the offshore dive sites. Few reds, but Flustra foliacea appears and bryozoan turf gets thicker. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Tops of outcrops had tatty kelp, the plants smaller than 1 m were overgrown with Obelia spp. and Membranipora mambranacea. The stipes were encrusted with Electra pilosa and Halichondria papnicea and red algae. Areas between the kelp with a range of turf species and encrusting species. There were large patches of Myxilla incrustans and Halichondria panicea. Other species included Clavelina lepadiformis, Aplidium spp. and Botrylus schlosseri common smaller species included Crisia spp. and Hiatella arctica. Noticeably fewer Alcyonium digitatum and Flustra foliacea than in deeper sites and more sponges. The sides of the outcrops had fewer algae than elsewhere. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: The habitat was uniform throughout the dive (approx. 250 m in length). The chalk humps raised approximately 250 - 500 mm above the hollows. 100% covered with mainly Polyclinum aurantium, with tufts of Flustra foliacea common: also seasquirt Archidistoma aggregatum and hydroids and bryozoans in patches especially around the side of the humps. The hollows were a mixture of chak bare bedrock with piddocks (approx. 10/0.5 x 0.5 m): also a covering of silt tubes of Polydora spp. (and possibly Fabricia sabella and Cliona celata boring. These hollows had some small stones with Pomatoceros spp. and Ophiothrix fragalis. The minature reefs had overhangs with edible crabs, lobster and Galathea strigosa. Any vertical surface (only 10 cm ! usually) with brittle star limbs out of holes (probably Ophiactis balli ). No algae were seen. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Rock outcrops up to 6 m high. Sides are vertical, steep sloping or overhanging. Typically lowest half metre rounded and scoured clean by wave and cobble action. Rock strata horizontally bedded providing lots of deep clefts up to 2 m iny and 30 cm high. Often runnuing continuously along considerable legnths of rock. Tops of rock, kelp forest with foliose red algal understorey. The upper vertical faces of rock were dominated by the foliose algae. Upper overhanging face had a rich faunal turf - unusually for the area based on sponges esp. Halichondria panicea. Rocks riddled with holes, many occupied by Hiatella arctica or Ophiothrix fragilis. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Aplidium proliferum: The main difference at this site was the abundance of Alcyonium digitatum - greater than in other areas; possibly due to the greater stability of rocks here. Some Polyclinum aurantium and condsiderable patches of Archidistoma aggregatum - as at other sites. Flustra foliacea also abundant on current swept surfaces. No sponges recorded except for Cliona spp. Turf difficult to distinguish much, but Halecium spp. and Bugula plumosa prominent, also Clavelina lepadiformis. Vertical surfaces silty - less colonisation. On low lying bedrock there were often areas densely packed with a >cm thick mat of tubicolous amphipods. Sometimes uninterrupted; or with algae/small hydroids showing through. Very silty. Urticina spp. protruding in places. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Horizontal surfaces of large boulders and bedrock. Irregular bedrock with shallow gullies in kelp forest. Understorey of red weeds e.g. Delessaria sanguinea nad large patches of Polyclinum aurantium. This soon gave way to more broken ground with deeper gullies and vertical edges of bedrock with fewer red weeds and a very rich animal turf. All species recorded for the area were present with no obviuos habitat preference. Occasional flat topped plateux, about 1 - 2 m above the general bottom and had sparse kelp cover with a more varied understorey of red weeds and faunal turf. Undercut edges had numerous crabs and a few lobsters and also one leopard spotted goby. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Rock platform, below reef face with polychaete tubes, mainly Sabellaria spinulosa and silt. Reef face a mixture of bryozoan /hydroid turf either Alcoynium digitatum or Flustra foliacea in dense stands. Bryozoan turf slightly different with Eucratea loricata and Vesicularia spinosa joining Scrupocellaria spp. and Bugula spp. Small stones covered with Cliona spp. which appears to prefer this to the bedrock. The stones were also covered with Pomatoceros and the encrusting bryozoan Escharella immersa. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Small rocks were covered with algae, mixed with some Halecium spp. Flatter rocks were covered with Polydora spp. and silt. Clavelina lepadiformis forms many small clumps on many small rocks. There were pebbles with Pomatoceros spp. Sand and gravel was colonised with Sabella spp. and Lanice conchilega (but not very many). Boulders have more bryozoa (Flustra foliacea and small tufts of Bicellaria ciliata, Crisia spp. and Bugula spp.) than red algae. Aplidium proliferum: Flat topped platform dissected by surge gullies generally about 600 mm deep and 1.5 m wide. Flat areas dominated by Flustra foliacea, bryozoan turf, Polyclinum aurantium and Cliona spp. The shallow sided gullies had Alcyonium digitatum common on the top edges, otherwise similar cover to the flat areas. Rounded gully bottom - with no life and clean of sediment. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Bedrock with widely separated terraces and much rock debris. The horizontal surfaces were densely fringed with Flustra foliacea (30 - 60 cm from the edges of outcrops and terraces). The other horizontal areas had a more mixed turf of Crisia spp., Leucosolenia spp. and Polyclinum aurantium. Vertical and more steeply sloping faces similar but generally more Bugula spp., Clavelina lepadiformis and Diphasia spp. Areas of freshly broken rock often but not always, exposed piddocks and had numerous crabs Cancer pagurus and a few squat lobsters Galathea strigosa. Faunal turf generally less dense than in other sites dived in this region, with areas of uncolonised, or thinly colonised rock, usually with boring Cliona spp. visible. Smoe smaller unstable stones covered with Pomatoceros spp. and prawns underneath. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Aplidium proliferum: Kelp forest at 1.3 m acd. Laminaria hyperborea common with a carpet of red algae underneath. Reds mainly Polysiphonia, species again mainly covered with Modiolus modiolus, Furcellaria lumbricalis found in areas where there was much sand accumulated. In general the rock was covered with a thin film of coarse sand. The kelp stipes, were covered with Palmaria palmata (common) along with Polysiphonia sp., Ceramium rubrum, Membranoptera alata and encrusting algae. The vertical surfaces supported algae towards the top but faunal turf dominated 75% of the cover. Amongst this turf a few filamentous weeds were found. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Low flat platforms of chalk with dense Polydora spp. turf and large patches of Taonia atomaria, with occasional Delesseria, Hypoglossum hypoglossoides, Plocamium acrtilagineum and Audouinella spp. Also sand with Sabellaria spinulosa. There was a kelp forest with a dense understorey of foliose algae. Polydora spp. in silt; Urticina felina in the crevices formed by sculpturing. Vertical faces were dominated by bryozoans (plus some sponges and Aplidium punctum), the ledges again were dominated by Polydora spp. Further offshore flat reefs, with the fauna gradually becoming similar to that of the offshore dive sites. Few reds, but Flustra foliacea appears and bryozoan turf gets thicker. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Aplidium proliferum: Thin kelp forest at 0.5 - 2.5 m with some red algae, which was not very profuse. Holdfasts generally small with Crisia spp. and Botryllus schlosseri being the main inhabitant. Other fauna on horizontal surfaces tended to be sparse and erratic, these species included Tubularia spp., Alcyonium digitatum and Botryllus schlosseri. The vertical components of the bedrock had a rich and varied animal turf with species including Bugula spp. and Clavelina lepadiformis. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Tops of outcrops had tatty kelp, the plants smaller than 1 m were overgrown with Obelia spp. and Membranipora mambranacea. The stipes were encrusted with Electra pilosa and Halichondria panicea and red algae. Areas between the kelp with a range of turf species and encrusting species. There were large patches of Myxilla incrustans and Halcihondria panicea. Other species included Clavelina lepadiformis, Aplidium spp. and Botrylus schlosseri common smaller species included Crisia spp. and Hiatella arctica. Noticeably fewer Alcyonium digitatum and Flustra foliacea than in deeper sites and more sponges. The sides of the outcrops had fewer algae than elsewhere. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: The main difference at this site was the abundance of Alcyonium digitatum - greater than in other areas; possibly due to the greater stability of rocks here. Some Polyclinum aurantium and condsiderable patches of Archidistoma aggregatum - as at other sites. Flustra foliacea also abundant on current swept surfaces. No sponges recorded except for Cliona spp. Turf difficult to distinguish much, but Halecium spp. and Bugula plumosa prominent, also Clavelina lepadiformis. Vertical surfaces silty - less colonisation. On low lynig bedrock there were often areas densely packed with a >cm thick mat of tubicolous amphipods. Sometimes uninterrupted; or with algae/small hydroids showing through. Very silty. Urticina spp. protruding in places. Ahnfeltia plicata: Wave cut platform in chalk with low ledges. Main feature of this habitat was the rich faunal turf on cobbles and boulders. Good diversity and % cover of bryozoans (esp. Flustra foliacea, Bugula spp. and Bicellaria spp.), hydroids (Halecium ciliata and Campanularia verticillatus) and tunicates (including Clavelina lepadiformis, Polyclinum aurautium, Botryllus sclosseri, Botrylloides leachi, with lesser quantities of Aplidium spp. and Sidnyum spp.). A few songes including Leucosolenia spp. on rocks, Cliona spp. boring into rocks and Polydora spp. evident in bare patches of bedrock. Alcyonidium diaphanum was abundant on most substrates especilly on bare rock. Mobile fauna was mainly Asterias rubens and crabs (Cancer pagurus was very common) and suprisingly Carcinus maenas (2 specimens were seen). NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

The sides of the boulders were covered in sea- squirts such as Botryllus schlosseri, Morchelium argus, Aplidium prolieratum common. Also Crisia eburnea, Bugula flabellata and Halecium spp.. Ahnfeltia plicata: Cliff face covered in pink encrusting algae with Patella vulgata abundant and Semibalanus balanoides common. Corallina officinalis and Chondrus crispus frequent to common and a few Laminaria digitata. Modiolus modiolus common on the rock surfaces. Actinia equina and Urticina felina in gully at base of cliff. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the riginal substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: The whole site appeared to be one large single habitat, despite the small pockets of sediment as these did not appear to be colonised. Unstable pebbles also remained uncolonised, but most of the rocks were large enough to support a fairly dense turf of faunal species, especially the moderate sized boulders. Several species were conspicuous and common including Clavelina lepadiformis in lumps and masses, Alcyonidium spp. (single pieces, widespread especially on tops of rocks), Flustra foliacea (tops/sides) of the rocks. Amongst the hydroids Halecium spp. was obvious, and the larger stouter Abietenaria spp. Bryozoans were abundant often dominating the boulder surfaces. Apart from Flustra foliacea the species involved were Bugula flabellata and Bugula plumosa. The small blobby tunicate Archidistoma abundant. Other species seen sporadically included the boring phase of Cliona spp., especially on low, small boulders, one or 2 clumps of Alcyonium digitatum. Rarely Sabella spp., protruding from sides of small boulders or silty sand pockets. Urticina felina was occasionally seen, on the bedrock boulder floor. Amongst mobile fauna - the commonest were young Asterias rubens, Hyas spp. and several Cancer pagurus and Pleuronectes spp. Otherwise few fish, just Callionymus lyra. Crossasteyr seen, and several Janolus were seen feeding on Bugula spp. Algae virtually non existant - just a few unidentified reds. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Onl sparse algal cover at this site - Plocamium cartilagineum, Delessaria sanguinea, Taonia atomaria and others. Laminaria hyperborea - one single plant, small and on top of a boulder. One of the most prominant animals was Flustra foliacea, often almost completely covering the sides of boulders. The other was Polyclinum aurantium, occuring in dense mats in places. There was in many places a well developed faunal turf especially common were Bugula spp., Setularia spp., Clavelina lepadiformis and also hydroids including Halecium spp., Archidistoma aggregatum also common in places - mat like straggly Alcyonidium spp. especially on the boulder tops. The flat bedrock was noticeably less well colonised - considerably scouring must occur, but spionid worms in evidence; small silty tubes strewn on the rock surface. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Uppermost parts with a few kelp plants and some red algae, especialy Phyllophora spp., Schottera nicaeensis and Rhodymenia holmsii at the bases of the outcrops. The vertical surfaces were dominated by a faunal turf. Crisia spp. were abundant and covered substantial areas. Polyclinum spp. was also abundant. Aplidium punctum was noticeably more common than on horizontal surfaces . Aplidium proliferum was also common. Myxilla incrustans was also conspicuous in places. Bugula spp. present (especially Bugula turbinata). Few hydroid varieties but Alcyonium digitatum rare at this depth/site. Nuerous Ophiothrix fragilis hidden within the tops of the crevices. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Rock outcrop boulder, roughly 2 x 2 m. On one side the boulderof formed a crevice and overhang. Under here Bugula plumosa was present. The surface of the rock was covered with Halichondria panicea. A couple of Laminaria hyperborea plants were present along with a few reds Hypoglossum and Lomentaria spp. A few animals including Haliclona oculata, Flustra foliacea and Tubularia spp. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Small rocks were covered with algae, mixed with some Halecium spp. Flatter rocks were covered with Polydora spp. and silt. Boulders have more bryozoa (Flustra foliacea and small tufts of Bicellaria ciliata, Crisia spp. and Bugula spp.) than red algae. Aplidium proliferum: Bedrock surfaces formed from various gullies, bedrock and small boulders. Occasional large boulders with sharply undercut edges in depressions in the bedrock. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Aplidium proliferum: Tops of outcrops had tatty kelp, the plants smaller than 1 m were overgrown with Obelia spp. and Membranipora mambranacea. The stipes were encrusted with Electra pilosa and Halichondria panicea and red algae. Areas between the kelp with a range of turf species and encrusting species. There were large patches of Myxilla incrustans and Halichondria panicea. Other species included Clavelina lepadiformis, Aplidium spp. and Botrylus schlosseri common smaller species included Crisia spp. and Hiatella arctica. Noticeably fewer Alcyonium digitatum and Flustra foliacea than in deeper sites and more sponges. The sides of the outcrops had fewer algae than elsewhere. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: The habitat is divided into niches, bedrock at reef face covered with extensive Polydora spp. The reef face and edges consist of dense stands of Alcyonium digitatum on Flustra foliacea, with bryozoan and hydroid turf interspersed. Small stones bored by Clioa spp. and the large boulders dominated by Flustra foliacea. The turf is mainly Crisia eburnea and Scrupocellaria scruposa. Ahnfeltia plicata: Rock outcrops up to 6 m high. Sides are vertical, steep sloping or overhanging. Typically lowest half metre rounded and scoured clean by wave and cobble action. Rock strata horizontally bedded providing lots of deep clefts up to 2 m in and 30 cm high. Often runnuing continuously along considerable legnths of rock. Tops of rock, kelp forest with foliose red algal understorey. The upper vertical faces of rock were dominated by the foliose algae. Upper overhanging face had a rich faunal turf - unusually for the area based on sponges esp. Halichondria panicea. Rocks riddled with holes, many occupied by Hiatella arctica or Ophiothrxi fragilis. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Low flat platforms of chalk with dense Polydora spp. turf and large patches of Taonia atomaria, with occasional Delesseria, Hypoglossum hypoglossoides, Plocamium cahrtilagineum and Audouinella spp. Also sand with Sabellaria spinulosa. There was a kelp forest with a dense understorey of foliose algae. Polydora spp. in silt; Urticina felina in the crevices formed by sculpturing. Vertical faces were dominated by bryozoans (plus some sponges and Aplidium punctum), the ledges again were dominated by Polydora spp. Further offshore flat reefs, with the fauna gradually becoming similar to that of the offshore dive sites. Few reds, but Flustra foliacea appears and bryozoan turf gets thicker. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: Rock platform, below reef face with polychaete tubes, mainly Sabellaria spinulosa and silt. Reef face a mixture of bryozoan /hydroid turf either Alcyonium digitatum or lFustra foliacea in dense stands. Bryozoan turf slightly different with Eucratea loricata and Vesicularia spinosa joining Scrupocellaria spp. and Bugula spp. Small stones covered with Cliona spp. which appears to prefer this to the bedrock. The stones were also covered with Pomatoceros and the encrusting bryozoan Escharella immersa. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.

Ahnfeltia plicata: The habitat was uniform throughout the dive (approx. 250 m in length). The chalk humps raised approximately 250 - 500 mm above the hollows. 100% covered with mainly Polyclinum aurantium, with tufts of Flustra foliacea common: also seasquirt Archidistoma aggregatum and hydroids and bryozoans in patches espcially around the side of the humps. The hollows were a mixture of chalk bare bedrock with piddocks (approx. 10/0.5 x 0.5 m): also a covering of silt tubes of Polydora spp. (and possibly Fabricia sabella and Cliona celata boring. These hollows had some small stones with Pomatoceros spp. and Ophiothrix fragalis. The minature reefs had overhangs with edible crabs, lobster and Galathea strigosa. Any vertical surface (only 10 cm ! usually) with brittle star limbs out of holes (probably Ophiactis balli ). No algae were seen. NOTE: Substratum % data are converted from the original substratum 1-3 scoring system.
